title: summarize_messages treats a mid-turn steer note as a turn boundary, compacting away the real question
description: core/state.py:13-17 documents the contract: everything that slices the conversation by 'last HumanMessage' must skip standalone steer notes via is_steer_message, 'or a steered turn gets mis-sliced: the steer note mistaken for the question, the real question compacted away.' Four slicers comply (agent.py:239 _compact_history, commands/conversation.py:162 and :290, nodes/ground.py:79) — but summarize_messages' boundary scan (compaction.py:78-80) filters only `not is_summary(m)`, not `is_steer_message(m)`. Reachable path: steering after a tool round makes plan_gate append a STANDALONE steer HumanMessage (nodes/plan_gate.py:60 — the merge branch only fires when the trailing message is already a HumanMessage, i.e. before the first tool round). _maybe_autocompact (agent.py:1073, fires past runtime.compact_threshold — exactly the long sessions where steering happens) and /compact (commands/conversation.py:120) then call summarize_messages on a history whose last turn still carries that steer note. With keep_recent_turns=1, boundary = human_idxs[-1] = the steer note's index, so the turn's REAL question plus its pre-steer scratchpad are folded into the LLM summary while the steer note survives as the apparent current question. Worse, the next turn's _compact_history then finds zero real-turn HumanMessages in the kept slice (it correctly excludes both summary and steer), so the verbatim question is gone from messages entirely — it survives only as lossy summary prose. Existing tests cover steer handling for _compact_history (tests/test_compact_history.py:54-63) but not for summarize_messages (tests/test_core.py:200-240), so nothing catches this.
fix: In core/compaction.py, import is_steer_message from core.state (no cycle — state.py imports nothing from compaction) and add `and not is_steer_message(m)` in BOTH comprehensions: (a) the boundary scan at lines 78-80, and (b) the stats count at lines 99-101 (`summarized_turns`) — otherwise a steer note in the older slice over-counts in the user-facing "auto-compacted N earlier turn(s)" notice once steered turns correctly fold. Steer notes in the older slice still render fine in the summary transcript as "User:" lines via _role. Add a test next to test_core.py:200 mirroring test_compact_history's steer case, but it MUST include at least one OLDER turn before the steered one (e.g. [Human(q1), AI(a1), Human(q2), AI(tc), Tool, Human(STEER_PREFIX...), AI(final)] with _llm_summary stubbed): with only the steered turn present, len(human_idxs) <= keep_recent_turns makes summarize_messages a no-op and the test proves nothing. Assert the boundary lands on q2 (q2 + its tool scratchpad + the steer note kept verbatim after the summary head) and summarized_turns == 1.

title: Glass Box taint corpus includes the model's own tool-call repr, producing false bold-red 'TAINTED -> answer' alarms
description: In `_assemble`, the untrusted-span corpus is built from the numbered source entry minus only the `[n] ` prefix: `obs = text[len(prefix):]` (line 215) then `untrusted_obs.append(obs)` (line 228). For tool sources, that entry is `nodes/tools.py:181`'s `f"{_fmt_call(name, args)} -> {clamped}"`, so the corpus contains the MODEL'S OWN arguments (`web_search(query='...')`, `web_extract(url='https://...')`), not just the untrusted observation. The data->action half (`quarantine.record_untrusted`, nodes/tools.py:164) records only `clamped` — yet quarantine.longest_overlap's docstring claims the two halves are 'judged identically'. Consequence: an answer that restates the search query (extremely common — e.g. query 'security implications of quantum computing for banking', 54 normalized chars >= _TAINT_MIN 40) or cites a long URL gets a tainted_span hit on the model's own text. Since the 2026-06-12 ambient wave, that renders natively on EVERY answer as bold red '⚠ untrusted content from web_search reached the answer verbatim' (tui/ui/response.py:167-188) plus '⛔ TAINTED -> answer' in the Sources footer and `/glass` — a recurring false alarm on the flagship trust surface. No test covers a long-query echo (tests use `query='weather'`/`query='x'`).
fix: Keep the proposed approach — it is the exact dual of nodes/synthesize._tool_source_label's existing parse of the same strings. In _assemble, before `untrusted_obs.append(obs)` (trust/glassbox.py:228), strip the call repr for TOOL sources only: `if idx < len(numbered_tools): obs = obs.split(" -> ", 1)[1] if " -> " in obs else obs` (doc sources carry no call repr and must stay whole). Note the residual edge honestly in a comment: an arg value that itself contains " -> " splits early and leaves some model-authored text in the corpus — fails toward caution and never drops genuine observation content, matching the label parser's identical assumption. Extend tests/test_glassbox.py with both directions: (a) an answer restating a 40+ normalized-char web_search query (observation unrelated) must yield tainted_span is None and gb.tainted == []; (b) the inverse guard — a planted span in the OBSERVATION of the same long-query entry must still set tainted_span (proving the split removed only the repr); and (c) the same via build_from_record, since _assemble serves both paths. Optionally fix the now-inaccurate "judged identically" wording in quarantine.longest_overlap's docstring to say both halves match against observation content only.

title: Gate 'a' (always-allow) decision vanishes from gate_events: policy mutation during the interrupt makes the node re-run recompute `gated` as empty
description: When the user answers `a` at the gate, `tui/ui/approval.py::_resolve_decision` (line 546-548) calls `_always_allow` BEFORE returning the resume value: it sets `registry.TOOL_RISK[name] = "read_only"` (line 495) and/or persists a run_shell /allow prefix via `_grant_shell_prefix`. `run_turn` then resumes with `Command(resume=True)` and LangGraph re-executes `approval_node` from the top. On the re-run, `gated` (approval.py:176-182) recomputes via `risk_of()`/`policy.approves()` against the NOW-MUTATED policy: every always-allowed tool auto-approves, so `gated` is empty and the node returns `Command(goto="tools")` at line 184-185 WITHOUT reaching `interrupt()` — the resume value is discarded unconsumed and, critically, the `gate_event(...)` recording site (lines 237-251) is never reached. The human WAS prompted and approved, but `state["gate_events"]` records nothing — violating gotcha #7's invariant ("an empty record always means the human was never prompted") for every consumer: headless `--json` gates, the Glass Box `gate_summary`, the signed export's `answer_attestation`, and the trace rail's '✓ you approved' echo (which reads the gate_events delta). Worse partial variant: if the batch mixed run_shell (user declines the prefix sub-prompt) with other tools (granted), the re-run's `gated` is smaller but non-empty — the second `interrupt()` consumes `True` against a DIFFERENT payload and the event records only the run_shell call, undercounting what actually faced the human. The quarantine-escalation case is unaffected (escalated keeps `gated` = all calls), so this fires exactly on the common tier-only gate. The node already documents the re-run-from-top hazard for the quarantine peek/consume dance (lines 151-158, 224-231) but the same hazard via the UI's policy mutation was missed.
fix:
The proposed direction (defer policy mutation past the interrupt; apply grants in approval_node's once-only post-interrupt region, alongside quarantine.consume_gate) is correct, but it is incomplete in three concrete ways:

1. Resume-shape collision: approval_node currently treats ANY dict resume as a per-call select — `approved_ids = gated_ids & set(decision.get("approved_ids") or [])` (approval.py:217-218) — so a `{"approve": true, "always": true}` resume without "approved_ids" would resolve to an EMPTY approved set, i.e. silently flip the user's 'a' into a full rejection. The decision-resolution branch must explicitly recognize the new shape (e.g. a dict carrying "always" with approve=True means all gated_ids approved) before the generic dict branch.

2. The run_shell sub-flow is interactive AND its validation mutates: keep the prefix proposal/typed-shorter-prefix sub-prompt in ask_approval (collect-only — it can keep pre-screening with the non-mutating policy.shell_prefix_rejects, as _propose_shell_prefix already does), and move the persist+verify+rollback currently in _grant_shell_prefix (add_shell_allow → shell_allowed verify → remove on mismatch) past the interrupt — preferably as a policy.grant_shell_prefix(prefix, command) helper in trust/policy.py so the trust mutation lives with the one policy store rather than in nodes/ or tui/. Note the node cannot print (design rule: diag, not print), so the grant disclosures either render at decision time in the UI in future tense ("will always-allow…") or ride the rail; a typed prefix that fails post-interrupt validation degrades safely (the command simply re-gates next batch) but should be diag-logged.

3. Test shape: stubbing `interrupt` as a returning function (the existing test_gate_events.py pattern) CANNOT reproduce this bug. The regression test must simulate re-run-from-top: either a minimal compiled StateGraph with a checkpointer driven via invoke + Command(resume=...), or invoke approval_node twice — first call's monkeypatched interrupt raises after capturing the payload, apply the TOOL_RISK/allowlist mutation exactly as _always_allow does today (reproducing the bug pre-fix), second call's interrupt returns the resume value — asserting exactly ONE gate_event listing every originally-prompted call as approved, plus a case asserting the partial run_shell-declined variant records the full original batch, not just the leftover.

title: calculate's pow() builtin bypasses the _MAX_POW_EXP guard — resource bomb
description: _MAX_POW_EXP (10_000) is only enforced in the ast.Pow BinOp branch (lines 56-61). The pow builtin is also exposed via _ALLOWED_FUNCS (line 25) and invoked unguarded through the ast.Call branch at line 76 (_ALLOWED_FUNCS[node.func.id](*args, **kwargs)). So while '9**99999999' is rejected, calculate('pow(9, 99999999)') computes a ~95-million-digit integer with the 2-arg builtin and hangs/OOMs the turn — exactly the failure the module comment (line 40, '9**9**9**9') claims to prevent. The _MAX_EXPR_LEN=1000 guard does not catch it (the expression is tiny). This is a read_only tool, so it runs without the approval gate.
fix: The proposed `abs(args[1]) <= _MAX_POW_EXP` check is incomplete: it only catches the positional 2-arg form. `pow(9, exp=20000)` parses to args=[9], keywords=['exp'] and `pow(base=9, exp=20000)` to args=[], keywords=['base','exp'] — both bypass an args[1] check (IndexError/absent) and I confirmed both still compute the bomb. Two robust options: (1) Simplest and fully closing — drop 'pow' from _ALLOWED_FUNCS (the ** operator already covers exponentiation with the existing guard; the 3-arg modular form is niche). (2) If keeping pow for the cheap 3-arg modular form, in the ast.Call branch special-case func.id=='pow': resolve the exponent from BOTH positional and keyword args (positional index 1, or keyword 'exp'), and when no modulus is supplied (no 3rd positional arg and no 'mod' keyword) enforce abs(exp) <= _MAX_POW_EXP before calling, mirroring lines 59-61. Add a test covering pow(9, 20000), pow(9, exp=20000), and pow(base=9, exp=20000) alongside the existing ** cases.

title: persist() over a mapping parent rewrites the section header and corrupts config.yaml so the app cannot boot
description: `/config web foo --save` (or `rag`, `runtime`, `shell`, `mcp`, `paths` — any section name) is unguarded end to end. commands/config.py:258 calls `cfg.set("web", "foo")`, which replaces the whole `web` dict with a scalar in memory (Config.set, config.py:114-120, has no isinstance guard). `_persist_key` then calls `persist("web")`: the value is now a scalar so the `isinstance(value, (dict, list))` check at config.py:297 passes, and `_set_yaml_scalar` matches the bare `web:` section-header line (config.yaml:125), rewriting it to `web: foo` while its indented children (`provider: auto`, `max_results: 5`, …) remain in place. The result is unparseable YAML (`mapping values are not allowed here`): `_load()` runs at module import (config.py:349), so the NEXT launch of `python agent.py` dies with a yaml ScannerError before anything renders, and the user must hand-repair config.yaml. The persist even reports success (`saved to config.yaml`). Even without --save, the session's `web.*`/`runtime.*` lookups silently return defaults because Config.get finds a non-dict node.
fix: Two-layer guard, but place the set-side guard in the /config handler, NOT in Config.set: commands/runtime.py:58-63,83 (/models) legitimately replaces a {provider, model} dict role binding with a bare scalar model id via cfg.set, so a blanket dict-replacement refusal inside Config.set would break /models rebinding. (a) In _set_yaml_scalar (config.py), after the stack path matches the target, raise KeyError("<key> is a section header, not an editable scalar leaf") when the after-colon content (inline `# comment` stripped) is empty AND the next non-blank/non-comment line is more deeply indented — this keeps genuinely-null scalar leaves editable and hardens every persist() call site, honoring the function's existing docstring contract. (b) In the /config handler at commands/config.py:258, before cfg.set(key, value), refuse when isinstance(cfg.get(key), dict) with a message listing the child keys (e.g. "web is a section — set one of: web.provider, web.max_results, ..."); this also fixes the session-only silent-defaults half. (c) Extend tests/test_config_persist.py with the mapping-parent-header case (raises, file text unchanged) and the null-scalar-leaf-still-editable case, per CLAUDE.md's change-one-extend-the-suite rule.

title: Gate's always-allow prompt renders the shell command through markup-enabled Console.input — brackets are silently eaten or crash the gate
description: In the rich path, ask_approval hands `_console.input` (markup=True by default) to `_resolve_decision` (line 648), and `_always_allow` embeds the raw proposed prefix into that prompt (line 514: `always-allow this exact command as a prefix? "{proposal}"`). The policy metachar screen (`trust/policy.py:44`, `[;&|<>`$\n\r]`) does NOT reject `[`/`]`, so a legitimate command like `grep [error] app.log` reaches this prompt. Verified live: rich renders it as `grep  app.log` — the bracket token is silently deleted from the very text the user is confirming as a persistent gate exemption; and a `[/...]`-shaped token (e.g. `ls [/tmp/x]`) raises rich.errors.MarkupError, killing the turn mid-gate after the user already typed `a`. This directly contradicts the file's own byte-faithful contract (`_wrap_exact`: "the only transformation allowed is the line break itself") — the displayed grant differs from the stored grant. `prompt.ask()` already passes markup=False for exactly this reason.
fix: At tui/ui/approval.py:648, pass a render-faithful wrapper instead of the bare handle: `decision = _resolve_decision(resp, tool_calls, lambda p: _console.input(p, markup=False, emoji=False))`. markup=False stops brackets in the proposed prefix being eaten as Rich tags or raising MarkupError; emoji=False additionally stops `:name:`-shaped tokens in the command being substituted with emoji glyphs in the very text the user is confirming (Console.input defaults both on). The main gate prompt at approval.py:642-644 keeps its intentional [bold] markup unchanged; the plain-input path at :695 and the lambda-stubbed tests are unaffected. Optionally add a regression test in tests/test_gate_ux.py that drives ask_approval's rich path (or asserts the wrapper's kwargs) with a command containing `[error]` and `[/x]` to pin the contract.

title: /undo executes the destructive revert on any unrecognized argument (a typo'd 'list' restores files)
description: _undo only special-cases `args and is_list_verb(args[0])`; every other argument falls through to `snapshots.undo_last()`. So `/undo lst`, `/undo lis`, `/undo 2`, or `/undo show` — all plausible attempts to LIST batches — immediately pop the newest snapshot batch and overwrite workspace files with their turn-start bytes. There is no redo (deliberately deferred), so the turn's writes are unrecoverable. This is exactly the trap the repo already fixed for /mcp ('a typo'd relod must error, not silently render status'), but here the silent default mutates files instead of printing a readout.
fix: After the list-verb branch, guard the fall-through: `if args: _print(f"  unknown argument {args[0]!r} — usage: /undo [list]  (bare /undo reverts the last writing turn)"); return` so only a bare `/undo` performs the revert. Extend the /undo coverage in tests to pin it.

title: /trace replay and /trace calls misattribute tool outputs when a batch mixes retrieval and non-retrieval calls
description: _enrich_results (tui/ui/trace.py:355-368) pairs tool_events with tool_results purely by index: `results[i]` for `events[i]`. But nodes/tools.py routes retrieval observations into documents_retrieved instead of tool_results (line 178-181), while tool_events records EVERY call. So in a batch like [search_knowledge_base, web_search], events has 2 entries and tool_results has 1: the replay (show_run -> _render_tool_events(always_show_results=True)) prints web_search's full observation under the search_knowledge_base branch — wrong output attributed to the wrong call in the audit drill-down. commands/trace.py:_calls (lines 65-67, 81-85) has the same positional pairing, worse: call_repr comes from results[i] but the ok-glyph/duration come from ev, so a mixed batch shows web_search's repr with search_knowledge_base's duration and the retrieval call vanishes from the listing. Side effect even without mixing: retrieval outputs are never enriched at all — a RAG-heavy run replays with only the 160-char tool_events preview despite the same delta carrying the full text in documents_retrieved, contradicting the docstring's 'shows real output where the live tree deliberately showed nothing'.
fix:
Two-cursor walk keyed on RETRIEVAL_TOOLS as proposed, but account for the SHAPE difference between the two streams: tool_results entries are "name(args) -> obs" strings, while documents_retrieved entries are the raw clamped passage with NO call-repr prefix.

1) tui/ui/trace.py:_enrich_results(events, results, docs, cap=1200): lazy-import RETRIEVAL_TOOLS inside the function (glassbox._assemble's exact pattern — tui must not pull tools.registry/mcp startup at import time). For each event: if ev name in RETRIEVAL_TOOLS, consume the next docs entry and use it WHOLE as the result (do NOT partition " -> " — partition on a passage without the separator yields an empty observation and silently keeps the preview); else consume the next results entry and partition " -> " as today. Cursor exhaustion on either side falls back to the event's own preview (existing behavior). show_run passes delta.get("documents_retrieved") or [] alongside delta.get("tool_results") or [].

2) commands/trace.py:_calls: same per-delta two-cursor split, but for retrieval rows do NOT run the partition — build call_repr from the event (f"{ev.get('name','?')}({fmt_args(ev.get('args', {}))})", fmt_args already imported) and use the docs entry as the observation; non-retrieval rows keep the existing partition-with-fallback. This both fixes the duration/repr cross-wiring and stops the retrieval call vanishing from the listing.

3) Tests: extend tests/test_replay_and_source.py (or a new test module) with a mixed-batch delta {tool_events: [skb, web_search], tool_results: ["web_search(q=..) -> WSOBS"], documents_retrieved: ["[source: notes.md] DOCTEXT"]} asserting _enrich_results gives the skb event DOCTEXT and the web_search event WSOBS; cover both orderings ([skb, web_search] and [web_search, skb]) and a retrieval-only round (docs consumed, no preview fallback). Mirror a case through _calls asserting the retrieval row renders search_knowledge_base's name with its own duration.

title: Recorded final answer silently truncated at 2000 chars — /trace replay, /glass #id, and the signed export all render a cut answer with no marker
description: Tracer.end_run stores `(response or '')[:2000]` (stores/trace.py:163). Every after-the-fact surface reads runs.response: show_run's 'final answer (recorded)' block (tui/ui/trace.py:445-460) prints a mid-sentence cut with no elision marker; _export_markdown's '## Final response' and the JSON export's run.response carry the cut text into the shareable audit artifact; and glassbox.build_from_record taint-scans the TRUNCATED answer, so an untrusted span that bled into the tail of a long answer is invisible to /glass #id and to the signed answer_attestation, while the live /glass (which reads the full state response) would flag it — the recorded and live trust surfaces diverge on exactly the headline claim. A long synthesized answer (multi-source research with a Sources footer) routinely exceeds 2000 chars, and the footer itself sits at the end, so even the [n]->source map can be lost from the record.
fix: Proposed fix is sound; sharpen it as a both-and plus one missed reader: (1) In Tracer.end_run, raise the cap to _DATA_CAP (16000) — consistent with the module's own earlier cap-bump rationale — and when text still exceeds it, append an explicit marker at write time (e.g. '\n… [recorded answer truncated at 16000 chars]'), so the stored row is self-describing; the export digest/signature then commit the marker honestly. (2) Readers detect the marker: show_run renders a dim '(truncated)' suffix; _answer_attestation (commands/trace.py:205) and the /glass #id path (:878) pass complete=False when the marker is present, reusing the existing INCOMPLETE rendering — no new vocabulary, exactly the _DATA_CAP honesty pattern. (3) Also covers /trace why's 'cited sources (from the answer)' block (commands/trace.py:798) automatically once the footer survives the higher cap. Notes: write-time changes cannot repair historical rows — old records stay ambiguous, matching the absent-as-unknown convention (gotcha #7 style), so do NOT try to infer truncation for legacy rows; the marker is shorter than the 40-char taint minimum and appended after _strip_footer's '\nSources:' search target, so it needs no special-casing in the taint scan. Extend tests in the same change (test_glassbox/test_replay_and_source): a >cap answer round-trips with the marker, complete=False propagates, and a tainted tail span within the new cap is caught by build_from_record.

title: Signed trust report attests session egress from a clearable ledger with no cleared marker — can attest 'sent: 0' over a session that sent
description: `build_report` embeds `"egress_session": egress.summary()` inside the signed body. `egress.summary()` aggregates the in-memory `_LEDGER`, which `/privacy egress clear` empties (commands/privacy.py:198 -> egress.clear()). egress.py deliberately tracks this exact hazard — `_CLEARED_AT`/`cleared_since` exist so 'truth-claiming surfaces' treat a clear-emptied slice as UNKNOWN, and receipt.turn_spans (trust/receipt.py:112) and glassbox.build_live (trust/glassbox.py:315) both honor it — but the trust report, the most truth-claiming surface of all ('here is what it actually did', signed and portable), never consults it. After a clear, `/privacy report -o` produces a digest+signature-valid artifact asserting e.g. `egress_session: {sent: 0, bytes: 0, hosts: []}` for a session that sent plenty. With `runtime.egress_log: false` the durable section can't contradict it, so the signed artifact's only activity record is silently understated.
fix: The chokepoint is right but the fix needs two additions. (1) Add the marker in egress.summary() — e.g. "cleared": _CLEARED_AT > 0 (optionally "cleared_through_seq": _CLEARED_AT for how much history was wiped) — which rides into the signed egress_session automatically; all current summary() consumers (privacy.py:213, privacy.py:456, trust_report.py:110, test_egress.py) read specific keys, so the new key is backward-compatible. (2) Disclose it in BOTH human renders: the /privacy report session block (commands/privacy.py::_report, the se = report["egress_session"] section ~line 613) and the /privacy egress headline (~line 213) should append something like "(ledger cleared this session — counts are since the clear; the durable log retains the full record)". (3) Tests: the proposed test ("report built after egress.clear() carries the cleared marker") is necessary but insufficient — the existing suites use egress.clear() as a test-isolation reset (test_egress.py's autouse fresh_ledger fixture; every test_trust_report.py test opens with clear()), so the flag would read True across them all. New assertions for the never-cleared case must reset the module state explicitly (monkeypatch.setattr(egress, "_CLEARED_AT", 0) — the pattern test_ambient_trust.py:335 already touches), and the isolation fixtures should reset _CLEARED_AT alongside the ledger so "fresh session" semantics stay testable. Add: summary()["cleared"] is False on a fresh session, True after clear(); build_report() after record+clear carries egress_session.cleared True inside the signed body (digest/verify still pass).

title: /policy allow and apply_profile store metacharacter prefixes unscreened; confirmation and /policy can then claim a gate exemption that can never match
description: `allow_handler` -> `_allow_add` calls `policy.add_shell_allow(prefix)` directly with no `shell_prefix_rejects` screen (commands/policy.py:139-201), and `policy.apply_profile` likewise stores any non-empty `shell_allow` entry after whitespace-normalizing it (trust/policy.py:253-257 — which even collapses newlines, laundering one class of metacharacter). So `/allow git status && rm -rf ~` stores successfully and prints 'commands starting with `git status && rm -rf ~` now skip the gate' — false: `shell_allowed` (trust/policy.py:323) rejects any COMMAND containing a metacharacter, so the stored prefix is permanently inert. `/policy can` then lists it under 'WITHOUT ASKING' with the note 'token-boundary, no metacharacters' (commands/policy.py:287-292). No security hole (fails safe), but two trust surfaces — the confirmation copy and the blast-radius readout — make statements the matcher contradicts, and the documented contract ('refuses to exempt any command containing shell metacharacters', module docstring) says such prefixes should be refused at the door, as the gate UI's always-allow flow already does via the public `shell_prefix_rejects`.
fix: Screen prefix candidates at both store entry points, using the existing public screen — never a second regex. (1) trust/policy.py add_shell_allow: ask shell_prefix_rejects on the RAW input before normalizing (normalization launders newlines into spaces, so screening after misses one metachar class) and raise ValueError with the returned reason; keep the bool return for duplicates. (2) commands/policy.py _allow_add: catch the ValueError and print the reason plus the existing rule ("a command containing ; & | < > ` $ or a newline always faces the gate — no prefix can exempt it"), storing nothing. (3) trust/policy.py apply_profile: it bypasses add_shell_allow (writes data["shell_allow"] directly), so add the same shell_prefix_rejects check to its validate-everything-first loop and raise ValueError naming the offending entry — consistent with its existing hard-error contract for risk tiers/threshold/redaction ("never half-applies"); keep silently dropping blank entries (tested behavior, test_policy_profiles.py:84-88). (4) tui/ui/approval.py _grant_shell_prefix: it currently passes a possibly-metachar user-typed prefix straight into add_shell_allow and relies on add-then-verify-rollback — pre-screen the typed prefix with policy.shell_prefix_rejects (or catch the new ValueError) and return the same (False, disclosure) shape, so tests/test_gate_ux.py:181-189 keeps passing with its current assertions. (5) Tests: extend test_policy.py (add_shell_allow raises on ; | & < > ` $ and newline, raw-before-normalize), test_policy_commands.py (/policy allow with a metachar prefix prints the refusal and stores nothing), test_policy_profiles.py (a profile with a metachar shell_allow entry raises ValueError and applies nothing), and adjust/extend test_gate_ux.py for the new _grant_shell_prefix path.

title: Grounding recap pairs each question with the agent's intermediate DRAFT, never the final synthesized answer
description: `_recent_exchanges` (ground.py:61-95) pairs `pending_q` with the FIRST no-tool AIMessage that follows it, then sets `pending_q = None` (lines 84-88), so later no-tool AIMessages in the same turn are ignored. But on essentially every normal turn the conversation carries TWO consecutive no-tool AIMessages: the agent's draft (the no-tool finish that routed to replan/synthesize) followed by synthesize's final answer — and `_compact_history` preserves both (both are non-empty, non-tool-call AIMessages), for the most recent turn AND for compacted older turns. Verified by repro: for [Human(q), AI(tool_calls), AI("DRAFT..."), AI("FINAL...")] the recap contains the DRAFT and not the FINAL. Consequence: the "Recent conversation" recap — the ONLY conversational view the planner and synthesizer get (they read `context`, not `messages`) — systematically shows the draft instead of the answer the user actually saw. Worst case is the replan-repair path: the recap carries the UNGROUNDED draft the judge rejected (the web_search results and corrected final answer never make it in), so a follow-up turn is planned against the very claims the trust machinery caught and fixed. tests/test_recap.py never exercises the draft+final shape (its turns have a single AIMessage answer), which is why this was missed.
fix: In nodes/ground.py `_recent_exchanges`, let a later no-tool AIMessage in the same turn supersede the pair's answer, but only when non-empty: after `if pending_q and answer: pairs.append(...); pending_q = None`, add `elif pairs and answer: pairs[-1] = (pairs[-1][0], answer)` — the `and answer` guard is required, or an empty trailing no-tool AIMessage overwrites a real final answer with "". Extend tests/test_recap.py in the same change (per CLAUDE.md's test rule) with: (a) the draft+final shape asserting the recap shows the final answer, (b) the replan-repair shape (ungrounded draft, inserted web_search tool round, grounded draft, final) asserting the rejected draft never appears, and (c) a trailing empty AIMessage after the final asserting the final answer survives.

title: _skip_rejected_steps fallback retires an UNRELATED later step when rejected calls outnumber the matching planned steps
description: The fallback at approval.py:79-88 fires whenever `sum(remaining.values()) > 0` after the main walk — i.e. whenever ANY rejected-call count was left unconsumed — not only when a rejection "didn't line up with any planned tool" (the docstring's stated intent, lines 47-50). When the agent issues two same-tool calls in one batch (common: two parallel run_shell commands serving one planned step) and the user rejects the batch, the main walk skips the one matching step, the leftover count (1) triggers the fallback, and — since the matching step is now terminal — the fallback skips the FIRST UNRELATED non-terminal step with an intended_tool. Verified by repro: plan [s1: run_shell, s2: write_file], rejected=[run_shell, run_shell] → BOTH steps come back 'skipped'. The user declined two shell commands and the plan silently retires the write_file step they were never asked about: lockstep stops driving it, `unrun_planned_tools` goes quiet (no nudge, no honesty note in synthesize), and the turn ends without ever attempting or disclosing the dropped planned work. Same mis-skip for a mixed batch (one matched rejection + one unmatched): the fallback's target is supposed to be the step lockstep was driving, but that step was already retired by the main walk, so it hits the wrong one. No test in tests/test_plan_accounting.py pins this scenario (the fallback tests use a purely-unmatched rejection), and this is distinct from the deferred walker-triplication item — it is a logic error inside this one walker.
fix: The proposed fix is correct and complete as stated. One strengthening note for the implementer: frame the new `skipped_any` guard as mirroring update_plan_node's `if called and not newly_marked` fallback condition (nodes/update_plan.py:62) — same semantics, same comment vocabulary — since gotcha #6 requires the walkers to keep identical accounting, and add the new two-rejected-same-tool-calls test to tests/test_plan_accounting.py alongside the existing _skip_rejected_steps tests (ideally with the update_plan_node/unrun_planned_tools cross-check the suite already uses).

title: write_file and edit_file register workspace-manifest entries under inconsistent keys
description: write_file registers the manifest entry under the raw model-supplied file_path (line 79: register_workspace_file(file_path, content), and line 87 for append), whereas edit_file uses str(target_path.relative_to(workspace)) (line 134), and snapshots.snapshot_file / undo_last canonicalize to as_posix() (snapshots.py line 102 / 197). For a file in a subdirectory on Windows these diverge: write_file keys 'notes/todo.md', edit_file keys 'notes\\todo.md', undo removes 'notes/todo.md'. Net effects: editing a previously-written subdir file creates a DUPLICATE manifest entry instead of updating it, and /undo deleting an edit-only entry (or removing after a backslash-keyed write) leaves a stale entry pointing at a file that no longer exists. The manifest is read into grounding context every turn, so the LLM sees phantom/duplicate documents.
fix: Normalize all three writers to one key form. In write_file pass target_path.relative_to(workspace).as_posix() (not the raw file_path) to register_workspace_file at lines 79 and 87; in edit_file change str(target_path.relative_to(workspace)) to target_path.relative_to(workspace).as_posix() at line 134, matching snapshots/undo.

title: web_search Tavily->DuckDuckGo fallback mis-attributes egress and records nothing for the DDG request
description: backend_host is computed once at line 157 from _use_tavily(); egress.record fires for that host at line 161 BEFORE the call. When _use_tavily() is True but the Tavily call then fails (dead key at line 166, or any transient error at line 168), control falls through to _ddg_search at line 174, which contacts duckduckgo.com but calls neither egress.check nor egress.record. Result: the egress ledger — the product's authoritative 'what left the machine' surface — records a tavily.com event that never completed and SILENTLY OMITS the real DuckDuckGo request. Air-gap is also evaluated only against tavily.com.
fix: Keep the single top-level egress.check (the air-gap gate keys on airgap_on(), not host — one check is correct and avoids double-recording blocked events). Move egress.record to the actual send points, recording BEFORE each network attempt to preserve the ledger's deliberate fail-toward-recording property (egress.record docstring; http_request does the same): (a) inside the `if _use_tavily():` branch, record host="tavily.com", provider="tavily" immediately before `_client().search(...)`; (b) immediately before the `_ddg_search(...)` call at line 174, record host="duckduckgo.com", provider="duckduckgo". In the transient-fallback case this correctly yields TWO events — both exits really happened. Recording at the line-174 call site (rather than inside _ddg_search) keeps the helper pure; either placement is acceptable. Do NOT add egress.check inside _ddg_search — redundant under the top-level gate. Per CLAUDE.md's change rule, extend tests in the same change (e.g. in tests/test_egress.py or a new test_web_fallback): monkeypatch tools.web._client to raise UsageLimitExceededError and DDGS to a stub, then assert the ledger carries a duckduckgo.com event (and the tavily.com event for the attempted call). Optional same-pattern companion: web_extract has the mirror mis-attribution — in forced provider:tavily mode it records the TARGET URL's host while actually sending the URL to api.tavily.com; fix with the same per-branch recording.

title: /policy allow stores metacharacter prefixes that can never match, then claims they skip the gate
description: `_allow_add` (commands/policy.py:199) calls `policy.add_shell_allow(prefix)` with no metacharacter screen (trust/policy.py:173 stores anything non-duplicate). But the matcher `policy.shell_allowed()` (trust/policy.py:316-332) first runs `shell_prefix_rejects(command)` on the COMMAND, so any command that could match a prefix containing `; & | < > ` $` or a newline is rejected wholesale — a stored prefix like `/allow echo $HOME` or `/policy allow git status && git push` is permanently inert. Yet `_allow_add` prints "run_shell commands starting with `…` now skip the gate" (false — they will always prompt), and `/policy can` (commands/policy.py:289-292) renders the same prefix under "WITHOUT ASKING" with the note "token-boundary, no metacharacters" — a false blast-radius claim on the product's flagship posture readout. The gate UI's always-allow twin already does this right: `tui/ui/approval.py::_propose_shell_prefix` (line 447) asks `policy.shell_prefix_rejects` — CLAUDE.md names that function THE public face of the screen — but the /allow command path never adopted it. Fail-closed, so no security hole, but a trust surface that misstates the live posture. `policy.apply_profile` imports such prefixes unscreened too.
fix:
The proposed fix is correct in direction; sharpen it in three places:

1. `_allow_add` (commands/policy.py:199): ask the public screen first — `reason = policy.shell_prefix_rejects(prefix)`; if truthy, print a refusal carrying the returned reason (e.g. "  `{prefix}` can never skip the gate — {reason}; chained/redirected commands always face the human. Nothing added.") and return without storing. This mirrors the gate UI's `_propose_shell_prefix` pattern (screen at the caller via the one public predicate; the store stays dumb) — do NOT move the screen inside `policy.add_shell_allow`, whose bool return currently means "duplicate?" and is relied on by `_grant_shell_prefix`'s rollback logic.

2. `apply_profile` (trust/policy.py:253-257): screen each shell_allow entry with `shell_prefix_rejects` during the validate-everything pass and raise ValueError, consistent with the function's documented all-or-nothing contract for invalid tiers/redaction (a profile claiming an exemption the matcher will never honor is exactly the posture-misstatement the hard-error rule exists for). If breaking imports of legacy profiles that already carry inert prefixes is a concern, a skip-with-report in the returned summary is the acceptable softer alternative — but silent import must end either way.

3. Tests (CLAUDE.md rule: change a load-bearing surface → extend the suite in the same change): add to tests/test_policy_commands.py that `/policy allow echo $HOME` and `/policy allow add x && y` refuse, print the reason, and leave `policy.shell_allow()` unchanged; add the profile-side case to tests/test_policy_profiles.py.

Optional (cheap, worth doing): pre-existing inert entries in an installed permissions.json will still render falsely in `/policy can` and `_allow_list` after the add-path fix — have those two readouts annotate any stored prefix that `shell_prefix_rejects` flags (e.g. "(inert — contains a shell metacharacter, never matches; remove it)") instead of listing it under WITHOUT ASKING.

title: undo_last() deletes the snapshot batch even when one or more restores FAILED, destroying the only copy of the user's pre-write bytes
description: In `undo_last`, each file restore is wrapped in try/except (lines 177-189): a failure (e.g. the target file held open/locked — common on Windows, the dev platform — or a permission error) appends `FAILED to restore {rel}` to the action list and `continue`s. The sandbox-skip branch (line 174-176) likewise just records `skipped`. But line 204 then runs `shutil.rmtree(batch_dir, ignore_errors=True)` unconditionally, deleting the saved turn-start bytes for the very files that were NOT restored. The user sees the FAILED line, releases the lock, runs /undo again — and there is nothing left to restore: the undo layer's safety copy was destroyed by the failed undo itself. This contradicts the module's best-effort contract (a snapshot exists precisely for the recovery case).
fix: In undo_last(), track per-entry outcome during the loop. If every entry restored/deleted cleanly, rmtree the batch as today. If any entry FAILED or was sandbox-skipped: (1) MANDATORILY rewrite the batch manifest to contain only the unresolved entries (the rewrite must not be optional — keeping the full manifest would make a retry re-restore already-succeeded files, clobbering any newer state, and the saved bytes for succeeded entries should also be pruned from files/ to match); (2) keep the batch and append an explicit action line like "kept this snapshot batch — run /undo again to retry the failed file(s)" so the handler surfaces retention; (3) since /undo always pops the newest batch and has no skip/drop affordance, a permanently-unrestorable entry would otherwise block the whole undo stack — either include the batch directory path in the message so the user can resolve/remove it manually, or add a `/undo drop` escape in commands/knowledge.py. Extend tests/test_snapshots_undo.py with a failed-restore case (e.g. monkeypatch shutil.copy2 to raise, or chmod the target read-only) asserting the batch survives, the manifest shrinks to the failed entry, and a second undo_last() after clearing the fault restores the file.

title: Workspace-manifest keys are written in three different path formats, producing duplicate entries and stale entries /undo cannot remove
description: `_upsert`/`_remove_entry` match blocks by the exact string `### {filename}\n`, but the three callers disagree on the key format for the same file: tools/files.py:79 and :87 (`write_file`) pass the RAW model-supplied `file_path` (`notes/draft.md`, `./draft.md`, or `notes\\draft.md` — whatever the model typed); tools/files.py:134 (`edit_file`) passes `str(target_path.relative_to(workspace))`, which is backslash-separated on Windows (`notes\\draft.md`); stores/snapshots.py:195/:197 (undo's manifest sync) passes the POSIX form (`notes/draft.md`). Concrete failure on Windows: write_file("notes/draft.md") creates entry `### notes/draft.md`; a later edit_file on the same file creates a SECOND entry `### notes\\draft.md` — the grounding manifest (read every turn by the ground node) now lists the file twice with diverging summaries. And /undo of a turn that wrote `notes\\draft.md` calls remove_workspace_file with the posix key, which misses the native-keyed entry, leaving the manifest permanently claiming a file exists after undo deleted it.
fix: Fix at both layers, but derive the canonical key from the already-resolved path, not PurePath: (1) In tools/files.py, have write_file register under target_path.relative_to(workspace).as_posix() (both call sites, :79 and :87) instead of the raw file_path, and change edit_file's str(target_path.relative_to(workspace)) to .as_posix() — target_path is already .resolve()d by _resolve, so this collapses "./", "..", and separators correctly, and matches exactly what snapshots.py already stores. (2) As defense in depth, normalize in register_workspace_file/remove_workspace_file with PurePath(file_path).as_posix() BEFORE _upsert/_remove_entry/_summarize so the summary cache shares the canonical key — but note PurePath does NOT collapse ".." components, which is why (1) is the load-bearing fix. (3) Do NOT touch register_rag_document alone: rag.sync keys register, remove, and its index.json consistently with str(path.relative_to(root)) (stores/rag.py:285/:406/:438); normalizing only the register side would desync remove_rag_document's manifest+summary-cache deletion. If symmetry is wanted, normalize register_rag_document AND remove_rag_document together and accept that existing native-keyed entries on Windows installs orphan until a /docs sync --force. Add a regression test (the current test_snapshots_undo.py neuters the manifest sync) asserting write_file→edit_file→undo on a nested path yields exactly one manifest entry that remove_workspace_file can delete.

title: sync() with full_rebuild never cleans up removed files' manifest entries — a deleted document stays in the grounding manifest forever
description: The removed-file cleanup loop (rag.py:402-408) iterates the `files` dict — but when `full_rebuild` is true (forced sync, embedder change, chunk_size/overlap edit, or a missing/corrupt vectors.json dump), `files` was just reset to `{}` at line 383, so the loop sees nothing and `remove_rag_document(source)` is never called for files that vanished from disk. Concrete path: user deletes `old.pdf` from database/documents/ and runs `/docs sync --force` (or changes `rag.chunk_size`, or the cache was wiped) — old.pdf's vectors are correctly absent from the fresh store, but its `.manifest.md` block and summaries.json entry survive. Because the new index.json `files` dict only records on-disk files, no FUTURE sync will ever notice the orphan either: the grounding node (which reads the documents manifest every turn) and the /docs table permanently list a document that no longer exists, until the manifest is hand-edited.
fix: In sync() (stores/rag.py), after computing `on_disk` (line 388), reconcile the MANIFEST against disk directly instead of (only) against the old index: iterate `manifest_entries(read_documents_manifest())` and for each entry whose name is not in `on_disk`, call `remove_rag_document(name)` and count stats["removed"]. This covers every full-rebuild trigger including a wiped cache/ dir, where index.json itself is gone and the old-index stale set is empty (the proposed fix's gap — cache/ is documented "safe to delete" and index.json lives there). Keep the existing incremental loop at 402-408 unchanged for vector deletion (store.delete needs chunk_ids from the old index; on the rebuild path the fresh store has no vectors to delete, so manifest/summary cleanup is all that's needed). Guard against double-counting a source removed by both passes (e.g. run the manifest reconcile only for names not already removed, or only on the full_rebuild path plus manifest-orphans). Add a regression test next to tests/test_rag_loaders.py: ingest two files (stub embedder per conftest isolated_paths), delete one from documents_dir, run sync(force=True), assert its `### <name>` block is gone from .manifest.md, its key gone from summaries.json, and stats["removed"] == 1; repeat with the cache dir deleted before the sync to pin the wiped-cache trigger.

title: Ctrl-C or Ctrl-D at the idle prompt crashes the REPL with an unhandled traceback
description: In main()'s interactive loop, `user_input = _next_input()` (agent.py:879) calls `ui.prompt()` (tui/ui/prompt.py:509-551), which -- unlike `ui.ask()` at prompt.py:565 -- has no KeyboardInterrupt/EOFError handling: prompt_toolkit's `PromptSession.prompt()` raises KeyboardInterrupt on Ctrl-C and EOFError on Ctrl-D, and the rich/plain `input()` fallbacks raise both. Nothing in main() guards the `_next_input()` call (the only KeyboardInterrupt handler is around `run_turn` at agent.py:973, inside the turn), so the most common quit habit -- Ctrl-C at an idle prompt -- kills the whole session with a raw Python traceback instead of a clean exit, and a closed/exhausted stdin (Ctrl-D, or `saturn < script.txt` running off the end of the file) does the same. The /quit autosave write (commands/system.py:143) is also skipped on this path (per-turn autosave limits the damage, but a turn-less session start ends in a stack trace).
fix: The proposed fix is directionally right; tighten it in four ways. (1) Guard only the `user_input = _next_input()` call at agent.py:879, not the whole loop body — `run_turn` already owns in-turn Ctrl-C semantics (agent.py:973) and a body-wide wrapper risks double-handling. (2) On EOFError, exit through the existing /quit path instead of duplicating it (e.g. `commands.dispatch("/quit", cmd_ctx)` then `break`, or call the same `commands.write_autosave(ctx.state)` + break) — the codebase's zero-drift rule is one handler with thin delegations, and `write_autosave` already skips empty conversations so a turn-less session exits silently and cleanly. EOFError MUST exit (never `continue`): an exhausted stdin would otherwise spin forever. (3) On KeyboardInterrupt, print the hint via `ui.note` (the existing dim-note vocabulary — design rule: never bare `print`) and `continue`; this matches `ui.ask`/the gate's "Ctrl-C is a soft no, never kills the session" behavior. (4) Per CLAUDE.md's test rule, add a test in the spirit of tests/test_gate_ux.py (monkeypatch `ui.prompt` to raise KeyboardInterrupt/EOFError and assert the loop continues/exits cleanly with autosave), since this change touches a covered UX surface.

title: benchmark.py never prunes its checkpoint threads -- every benchmark run permanently bloats production db.sqlite
description: The benchmark builds the real graph (`build_agent()`, checkpointed by SqliteSaver against DB_PATH = database/db.sqlite) and runs every query on a fresh thread_id -- run_query (benchmark.py:386-387), run_conversation (benchmark.py:328), and the warmup invoke (benchmark.py:431) -- but never calls `graph.checkpointer.delete_thread(...)`. agent.py's own loop prunes per turn precisely because otherwise checkpoints "accumulate in db.sqlite forever" (agent.py:1011-1020), and each checkpoint snapshots the full AgentState per node step, including ~12k-char clamped tool observations. A default trust run is ~9 queries and `--all` is ~45, each leaving a dead multi-checkpoint thread in the user's production database with no cleanup path (the per-turn prune only covers the interactive/headless loops), so repeated benchmark runs grow db.sqlite without bound.
fix: Mirror agent.py's best-effort prune at all three benchmark.py sites, in a finally so error paths prune too (matching the headless pattern at agent.py:747-751): (1) run_query — hoist `thread_id = str(uuid.uuid4()); config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": thread_id}}` above the existing try (it currently sits inside it at line 386) and add `finally: try: graph.checkpointer.delete_thread(thread_id) except Exception: pass`; (2) run_conversation — the config at line 328 is already outside the try; wrap the run_turn call's try/except with the same finally-prune (the finally fires before the error-path `break`, so a poisoned conversation still prunes its last turn); (3) _build_graph — capture the warmup thread_id in a local and call the same best-effort delete_thread immediately after graph.invoke(...). Optionally fold the uuid-config + prune pair into one small local helper to avoid writing the idiom three times.

title: Always-allow prefix prompt renders the shell command as Rich markup — misdisplays or crashes the gate
description: _always_allow() builds the scoped run_shell grant prompt as an f-string embedding the raw command (`ask(f'      always-allow this exact command as a prefix? "{proposal}" ...')`) and `ask` is `_console.input` (passed at approval.py:648), which parses the prompt as Rich markup by default. Empirically verified with the repo's installed rich: a command containing `echo done[/]` raises MarkupError (the exception propagates out of ask_approval, killing the whole turn after the user already pressed `a`); a command containing valid-looking tags (`awk "{print $1}" [bold]`, `ls [red]*[/red]`) has those tokens silently swallowed or styled, so the prefix the human SEES and confirms differs from the prefix actually granted to the policy allowlist — a divergence at exactly the safety-grant surface. Bracketed shell content (grep/sed character classes, globs, PowerShell `[string]` casts) is common. The codebase already knows this gotcha: prompt.py ask() passes markup=False with a comment about literal brackets.
fix: Don't escape at each embed site: `rich.markup.escape(proposal)` fixes the rich path but the identical f-string is also passed to builtin `input` in the plain fallback (approval.py:695 → _resolve_decision(resp, tool_calls, input)), where the inserted backslashes display literally (`echo done\[/]`) — a new misdisplay at the same surface — and it must be repeated for every future embed. Instead fix the single rich call site, matching the codebase's own convention (prompt.py:561-563): at approval.py:648 pass a markup-disabled callable — `decision = _resolve_decision(resp, tool_calls, lambda p: _console.input(p, markup=False))`. The prompts built inside _always_allow and _select_calls contain no intentional markup, so nothing is lost; this fixes both prompts in one place (including server-derived `mcp_*` tool names in _select_calls) and leaves the plain path's builtin `input` byte-faithful. Per CLAUDE.md's rule, extend tests/test_gate_ux.py in the same change with a bracketed-command case (e.g. `echo done[/]` and `ls *.[ch]`) exercised through a markup-parsing Console-backed ask callable, asserting no exception and that the rendered prompt contains the literal command text.

title: trace.py repeats the run-resolution + DB-fetch boilerplate five times
description: The same block — resolve the default run via `SELECT MAX(run_id)` with a "(no runs recorded yet)" message, fetch the run row, print "no run #id — try /trace -l", fetch events ordered by seq,id (and llm_calls behind the sqlite_master guard) — is hand-rolled in `_why` (lines 675-699), `_answer` (848-863), `_trace` (1036-1052), `_show_llm_calls` (1086-1128), and `export_run` (321-343), each with slightly different column lists that have already drifted (e.g. `_why` selects events without `ts`, `_answer` selects only `data`). The `#id`/bare-digit selector loop is likewise duplicated verbatim in `_why` (670-673), `_answer` (824-827), `_export` (400-403), and `_show_llm_calls` (1077-1082). Roughly 60-80 LOC of duplication in one module, and every future schema tweak must be made in five places.
fix: In commands/trace.py only, extract two narrower helpers that keep every per-site SELECT byte-identical instead of using superset columns: (1) `_selector_id(token) -> Optional[int]` wrapping the existing `token.startswith("#") or token.lstrip("+-").isdigit()` + `_to_int` idiom, called from inside each site's existing arg loop (_why, _answer, _export, _trace, _show_llm_calls) — do NOT extract a whole-args parser, because _export/_trace/_show_llm_calls interleave the selector with other flags and a bare digit means list-count in _trace's -l mode and doubles as the count in _show_llm_calls. (2) `_resolve_run(conn, run_id, *, run_sql, events_sql=None, calls_sql=None, default_sql="SELECT MAX(run_id) FROM runs", no_runs_msg=..., not_found_msg=...) -> (run_id, run, events, calls)` raising LookupError with the caller-supplied messages; each call site passes its CURRENT query strings (or column tuples formatted into the shared WHERE/ORDER BY skeleton), so positional unpacking in _final_plan/_collect_tools/_render_why (4-tuple events, 3-tuple calls), _answer (3-tuple run, 1-tuple events), and ui.show_run (6-col run, 5-col events — also the /trace replay contract) is untouched. _show_llm_calls passes default_sql="SELECT MAX(run_id) FROM llm_calls" and keeps its hard error on a missing llm_calls table (export_run/_why instead keep degrading calls to [] behind the sqlite_master guard — pass that distinction as a flag or leave the guard at those two sites). export_run keeps raising LookupError with its existing messages so test_cli's pinned contract (test_export_run_missing_run_raises_lookup, latest-run default) and the export payload are unchanged; REPL sites keep catching/printing their distinct hints. Run tests/test_cli.py, test_replay_and_source.py, test_gate_events.py, test_glassbox.py, test_signing.py, test_standalone_verifier.py after.

title: /trace invoke's truncation disclosure never renders — `--full` silently presents an 8000-char-capped message as complete
description: stores/trace.py caps each stored LLM message at `_LLM_MSG_CAP = 8000` and records the original length in `truncated` (stores/trace.py:188-190). show_llm_calls (trace.py:547-548) tries to disclose this: `body += f"  (+{m['truncated'] - _LLM_PREVIEW_CHARS} chars)" if not full else ""`. Three problems compound so the marker is dead code: (1) the math subtracts the UI preview clip (240) instead of the storage cap (8000), so the count would be wrong; (2) in the default view the suffix is appended to an ~8000-char body and then `_llm_leaf` clips to 240 chars, truncating the suffix away — it can never be seen; (3) in `--full` — the one view whose docstring promises "the entire stored message text" and where the user actually needs to know the store itself was capped — the suffix is explicitly the empty string. A trust-flagship inspection surface presents capped model I/O as if complete.
fix: In tui/ui/trace.py show_llm_calls (lines 547-548), replace the dead ternary with a storage-level disclosure that only renders in full mode, computed against the stored content (not the body, which already carries the tool_calls suffix): `if full and m.get("truncated"): hidden = max(0, int(m["truncated"]) - len(m.get("content") or "")); body += f"  [+{hidden} chars not recorded]" if hidden else ""`. Keep preview mode marker-free — its `…` clip already signals display truncation, and any tail-appended marker is unreachable there. The max(0, …)/int guard keeps the reader garbage-tolerant, matching the absent-as-unknown convention for trace readers. Apply the same treatment to the output side (line 551-558): the recorded output content is also capped at _LLM_MSG_CAP but carries no truncated flag at all — either add the flag in stores/trace.py:_llm_output (preferred, mirroring _msg_to_dict) or note the gap; without it --full also presents a capped output as complete. Per CLAUDE.md's test rule, extend the suite in the same change: a unit test (e.g. in tests/test_replay_and_source.py or test_core.py) building a calls row with a truncated input message and asserting the rendered full=True output contains `[+100 chars not recorded]` (original = _LLM_MSG_CAP + 100) and that full=False output does not.

title: Per-call select (`s`) prompts show only the tool name — two same-tool calls in a batch are indistinguishable
description: `_select_calls` asks `allow {name}? y / N` per call (line 531). When a batch carries two calls to the same tool — exactly the mixed-trust case `s` exists for, e.g. two `run_shell` commands or two `http_request`s where the user wants one and not the other — the prompts read identically (`allow run_shell? y / N` twice). The user must remember the listing order from the frame above, which may have scrolled past several diffs/wrapped arg blocks. At the safety gate, approving the wrong twin is a real hazard, and the rail's later `you approved/rejected run_shell, run_shell` echo can't distinguish them either.
fix:
In _select_calls (tui/ui/approval.py:531), include a clamped arg summary in each per-call prompt, escaping rich markup on the rich path so the fix is self-contained rather than dependent on the separate Console.input finding:

    for tc in tool_calls:
        summary = _base._fmt_args(tc.get("args") or {}, 48)
        if _RICH:
            from rich.markup import escape
            summary = escape(summary)  # ask is _console.input in rich mode — raw arg text would be interpreted as markup (silently restyled, or MarkupError mid-gate)
        r = ask(f"      allow {tc.get('name')}({summary})? y / N  (Enter = no) » ").strip().lower()

(ask is _console.input exactly when _RICH is true — see ask_approval lines 648/695 — so gating the escape on the module-level _RICH is correct.) If the markup-safe ask wrapper from the companion finding lands, drop the inline escape and rely on it. Extend tests/test_gate_ux.py::test_select_calls_enter_defaults_to_no (or add a sibling test) to assert the prompt passed to ask carries the arg summary, pinning the disambiguation. Optionally apply the same treatment to the existing unescaped embed at line 514 (_always_allow's prefix proposal), which has the same markup exposure today.

title: Gate prompt offers `s` and `a` with no explanation anywhere, and an unrecognized reply (e.g. `help`) silently rejects the whole batch
description: The prompt is `approve? y / N / s / a / e  (Enter = no)`. The first-gate preamble (lines 32-35) glosses only `e`; nothing in the UI ever says what `s` (per-call select) or `a` (always-allow, with the run_shell prefix subtlety) do — `a` in particular permanently relaxes the gate, which a user should not discover by experiment. Worse, `_resolve_decision` (line 551) maps ANY unrecognized input to reject, so a user typing `help` or `h` rejects the batch with zero acknowledgment at the prompt — the only feedback is the later rail echo `✗ you rejected …`, which records their confusion as a deliberate decision. Fail-closed is the documented contract (CLAUDE.md, test_gate_ux) and must stay; the missing piece is feedback and discoverability.
fix: Keep the fail-closed contract; add feedback in BOTH prompt loops via a small testable helper. (1) Add a module-level helper in tui/ui/approval.py, e.g. `_unrecognized_note(resp) -> str | None`: returns None for resp in the known set ("", "y", "yes", "n", "no", "a", "always", "s", "select", "sel") — e/explain/why/? never reach it (consumed by the loop's continue) — else returns the one-line note: `unrecognized "{resp}" — treated as no  (y approve · N reject · s per call · a always-allow · e explain)`. (2) In ask_approval, in BOTH the rich loop (after the explain check, before `_resolve_decision`, ~line 647) and the plain-fallback loop (~line 694), call the helper and, when it returns text, print it via `_frame_note(note, style=_DIM)` — the same post-prompt vocabulary `_render_explain` already uses — then proceed to `_resolve_decision` and break (reject stands; CLAUDE.md's "anything unrecognized rejects" contract is unchanged). (3) Open `_render_explain` with one dim `_frame_note` key legend line (`keys: y approve the batch · N reject (default) · s decide per call · a always-allow these tools this session (run_shell: scoped prefix grant) · e this explanation`) before the plan-step/reasoning lines — the preamble already directs users to `e`, so the legend becomes discoverable through the one key the preamble teaches. (4) Per CLAUDE.md's test rule, extend tests/test_gate_ux.py in the same change: `_unrecognized_note("help")` returns the note, `_unrecognized_note("")`/`("n")`/`("y")` return None, and the existing test_resolve_decision_default_rejects stays green (the helper never alters the decision).

title: /policy allow confirms a metacharacter-bearing prefix that can never match — a dead grant announced as live
description: _allow_add calls policy.add_shell_allow(prefix) which only whitespace-normalizes (trust/policy.py:173-181) — it never runs the shell_prefix_rejects metacharacter screen. Meanwhile shell_allowed (trust/policy.py:316-331) rejects ANY command containing ; & | < > ` $ wholesale, so a stored prefix like `echo ok | grep o` can never exempt anything. Yet the user sees "allowed: run_shell commands starting with `echo ok | grep o` now skip the gate." — a false confirmation — and `/policy can` then renders the dead prefix as a WITHOUT-ASKING row, misstating the blast radius. The user only discovers the grant is dead when the gate prompts anyway, with no explanation. The gate UI's own always-allow flow validates via policy.shell_prefix_rejects; the command path is the one entrance that skips it.
fix: The proposed fix is correct as written: in _allow_add (commands/policy.py:199), call reason = policy.shell_prefix_rejects(prefix) first and on a hit print a refusal naming the reason (e.g. "could not allow `{prefix}`: {reason} — chained/redirected commands always face the gate; they cannot be prefix-exempt") instead of storing; _allow_add is the shared chokepoint, so both /allow and /policy allow (bare and `add` spellings) are fixed in one edit. Add a case to tests/test_policy_commands.py asserting the refusal prints, policy.shell_allow() stays unchanged, and /policy can gains no row. Optional follow-up (separate entrance, same symptom): apply_profile (trust/policy.py:253-257) also stores shell_allow entries unscreened, so an imported profile can still seed dead prefixes into the /policy can WITHOUT-ASKING list — consistent with its hard-validation of tiers/redaction, either raise ValueError or screen-and-report those entries on import.

title: /clear wipes the conversation on a typo'd --screen flag instead of erroring
description: `screen_only = bool(args) and args[0].lower() in ("--screen", "-s", "screen")` — any OTHER argument (e.g. `/clear --scren`, `/clear screen-only`, `/clear -screen`) silently selects the destructive default and resets the whole conversation, the exact opposite of what the user asked for. The user wanted a repaint and lost their session context (recoverable only if they know to try /resume, which /clear's output never mentions). Unrecognized arguments to a destructive-default command should error, matching the /mcp precedent.
fix: In commands/conversation.py::_clear, after computing screen_only and BEFORE mutating state, reject unknown args using the /retry precedent from the same file: `if args and not screen_only: _print(f"  unknown argument {args[0]!r} — usage: /clear [--screen]"); return`. Additionally, in the full-reset branch (after the "new conversation" line), print the recovery pointer only when it is true: `if _autosave_file().exists(): _print("  (the previous conversation is still in the autosave — /resume restores it until your next turn overwrites the slot.)")` — _autosave_file is already imported at the top of the module. Per CLAUDE.md's "change one of those → extend the suite" rule, add a small test (tests/test_command_grammar.py is the natural home) asserting that /clear with an unrecognized argument leaves ctx.state's messages untouched and prints the unknown-argument usage line, and that "--screen"/"-s" still preserve the conversation.

title: /config silently 'sets' a typo'd dotted key (creates a dead key) and reads missing keys as '= None'
description: cfg.set (config.py:114-120) creates arbitrary nested keys via setdefault, and cfg.get returns None for a missing path. So `/config runtime.autoapprove read_only` (typo of auto_approve) prints `runtime.autoapprove = 'read_only'  (session only; add --save …)` — success-shaped output — while the gate threshold is untouched; the read form `/config runtime.max_iteratons` prints `runtime.max_iteratons = None` with no indication the key doesn't exist. On a trust-pitched product the config surface accepting a misspelled safety knob without comment is a real trap; /policy risk already sets the precedent with its difflib did-you-mean for unknown tool names.
fix: In commands/config.py's _config handler (not in Config.set — tests and internal callers use cfg.set directly and must stay permissive): probe existence with a sentinel (_MISSING = object(); exists = cfg.get(key, _MISSING) is not _MISSING). SET path (line ~258): when the key is absent, still perform cfg.set (hard refusal would break the documented default-tolerant knobs — shell.background, runtime.sign_exports, runtime.egress_log, paths.* — on a config.yaml predating them, plus new capabilities.<model>.* leaves) but replace the plain success line with a warning: "note: 'runtime.autoapprove' was not an existing config key — did you mean runtime.auto_approve? (set anyway; only keys the code reads have any effect)", using difflib.get_close_matches(key, flattened_leaf_keys, n=1) over a snapshot of dotted leaf paths walked from cfg._data BEFORE the set (so the typo'd key itself isn't in the candidate list). READ path (line ~247): when absent, print "<key> is not set" plus the same did-you-mean instead of "= None" (None remains the rendering only for a key explicitly present with a null value). Mirror /policy risk's exact suggestion wording ("— did you mean X?") for vocabulary consistency. Add tests in tests/test_command_grammar.py: (1) set of a near-miss key warns with the suggestion and does not touch the real key, (2) set of a genuinely new key still takes effect (default-tolerant knobs keep working), (3) read of a missing key prints "is not set", not "= None".

title: /trace why renders the judge verdict as raw structured-output JSON, or falsely claims the judge 'did not run' — contradicting /glass after a caught replan
description: _render_why takes the judge verdict from the replan llm_call's recorded `content` (line 769) and prints it verbatim (line 792). The judge is a structured-output model (llms.get_judge_model: with_structured_output(ReplanVerdict, method='json_schema') on Ollama, tool-forcing on cloud). On the default Ollama tiers the recorded content is the raw JSON object, so the flagship 'why' view prints `groundedness judge: {"grounded": false, "search_query": ...}` — unreadable on a polished trust surface. On a cloud-bound judge the verdict rides tool_calls and content is empty, so `judged` stays falsy and line 794 prints 'groundedness judge did not run — no ungrounded draft was caught.' even for a run where the judge DID run and DID insert a search — directly contradicting the same run's /glass label ('judge caught an ungrounded draft') and the rail's replan leaf, on the one surface whose comment says 'the Glass Box renders it, and why must match'.
fix: In commands/trace.py _render_why: (a) track judge-ran from the PRESENCE of a replan llm_call (keep the full `out` dict, not just content) — never from content truthiness; the no-replan-call case keeps the current "did not run" line, which also stays accurate for the replan node's skip path (web already searched / empty draft → judge never invoked, no llm_call recorded). (b) Derive the verdict by trying json.loads(content) first (Ollama json_schema), else the recorded out["tool_calls"][0]["args"] when it carries ReplanVerdict-shaped fields (grounded/search_query) — this also covers the Anthropic case where content is a non-JSON block-list repr. (c) Render sentences in the rail/glass vocabulary: grounded → "judge ruled the draft grounded — no search needed" (justified here, unlike glassbox, because the verdict record itself is in hand); ungrounded → "judge found the draft ungrounded — inserted a web search for \"<search_query>\"". (d) If a replan call exists but no verdict parses, fall back to the events cross-check the rail/glassbox already use (a replan delta carrying `replans` → render the ungrounded sentence; else "judge ran — verdict not recorded in a readable form"), so the line can never contradict /glass. (e) Per CLAUDE.md's test rule, extend tests/test_gate_events.py with realistic fixtures: Ollama-style content='{"grounded": false, "search_query": "..."}', cloud-style content="" + tool_calls=[{name: "ReplanVerdict", args: {...}}] (must NOT print "did not run"), and the no-call case unchanged.

title: Rail leaf claims 'judge: draft answer accepted' on turns where the judge never judged (skip and error paths)
description: _render_trust_annotations prints 'judge: draft answer accepted (no escalation)' for ANY replan delta without `replans` (line 164-167). But route_after_agent (nodes/agent.py:177) routes to replan on essentially every clean completion, and replan_node returns the identical empty delta on three different paths: (a) judge ruled grounded, (b) SKIPPED without invoking the model because web_search/web_extract/retrieval already ran or the draft was empty (nodes/replan.py:60-62 — i.e. every ordinary web-search or RAG turn), and (c) the judge call RAISED and the draft was accepted unverified (nodes/replan.py:79-82). So on the most common tool-using turns, the trust rail — live and in every /trace replay — affirmatively states the judge accepted a draft it never examined. That is exactly the class of overclaim the codebase polices elsewhere ('never "verified"', 'silence never implies local'), and the Glass Box already uses honest phrasing for the same 0-replans fact ('no ungrounded draft caught (judge ruled it grounded, or wasn't needed)').
fix: Reword the trace.py:167 leaf to assert only what the empty delta records, matching the Glass Box vocabulary: e.g. "judge: no ungrounded draft caught (ruled grounded, or wasn't needed)" — keep it _DIM, keep the {"replans"} branch unchanged. In the same change: (a) update tests/test_ambient_trust.py::test_replan_verdict_leaf_is_honest_both_ways, whose second assertion is `"accepted" in out` (assert on the new phrase, e.g. "no ungrounded draft"); (b) fix the _render_trust_annotations docstring (trace.py:156-158) so it no longer says the rail claims "accepted"; (c) update CLAUDE.md's quoted leaf text under the ambient-trust rail-annotations bullet ("accepted (no escalation)") plus a dated documentation.md changelog entry, per the project's on-every-feature-change rule. Treat the proposed optional outcome marker ("skipped"/"grounded"/"error" in the replan update) with caution: it adds a new persisted, partially non-recomputable state field (grounded-vs-error cannot be recomputed; the project deliberately keeps gate_events as the ONE such exception, gotcha #7), and any reader would need absent-as-unknown handling for pre-feature recorded runs — only add it if /trace why genuinely needs path attribution; the one-string reword alone fully resolves the overclaim.

title: Mid-turn steering corrections are invisible in the /trace drill-down — show_run skips plan_gate unconditionally
description: When the user steers mid-turn (Esc with text), plan_gate_node injects the correction as a HumanMessage and returns {'messages': [...]} (nodes/plan_gate.py:52-60); the tracer records that delta under node 'plan_gate' (summary '+1msg'). But show_run drops every plan_gate event before rendering (`if node == 'plan_gate': continue`, line 420-421), and _render_why never reads it either, so the replay shows the agent abruptly changing course with no record of the human input that caused it. For the product's audit story this is a real gap: a human's mid-run instruction is part of the causal chain (the rail's whole pitch is 'the transcript's permanent record'), the export's markdown timeline shows only '+1msg', and a recipient replaying a shared export cannot see why the trajectory bent. Live this is fine (the user typed it); only the recorded views lose it.
fix: In show_run (tui/ui/trace.py:420-421), decode the gate's own delta before rendering: replace `if node == "plan_gate": continue` with `if node == "plan_gate": _render_trace_messages(node, decode_json(data, {})); continue` (or move the existing `delta = decode_json(data, {})` line above the plan_gate check and pass that). _render_trace_messages quietly renders nothing for the empty pass-through/resume deltas, so ordinary gate events still fold away; a steer renders as an `in` leaf. Two caveats worth noting in the change: (1) in the merged-onto-trailing-HumanMessage case (steer at the first boundary) the recorded message is the full original query + "[STEER] …" note, so the leaf re-prints the query — acceptable, or filter the rendered content to the text from STEER_PREFIX onward when present; (2) optionally also render `show_plan(delta["plan"])` for a plan edited at the review interrupt (the same skip currently hides review-time plan edits), though that is beyond this finding's scope. Extend tests (e.g. test_replay_and_source.py) with a recorded plan_gate steer event asserting the replay output contains the steer text. Surfacing the steer in /trace why's chain is a reasonable optional follow-up but not required.

title: /trace invoke's '(+N chars)' truncation marker can never be seen, and --full silently hides that stored messages were cut at 8k
description: stores/trace._msg_to_dict stores at most 8000 chars per message and sets `truncated` = the original length only when content exceeded that (stores/trace.py:188-190) — common, since ToolMessages are clamped at ~12k. show_llm_calls appends the marker to the END of the ~8000-char body (line 548), then _llm_leaf clips the whole string to _LLM_PREVIEW_CHARS=240 (line 474-475) — so in the default view the marker is always truncated away before display. In --full mode the marker is suppressed outright (`... if not full else ""`), so the view the help text promises as 'whole messages' renders an 8000-char prefix with zero indication 4000+ chars are missing. Net: the truncation disclosure is dead code in both modes, on the surface whose job is 'what did each model call actually see'.
fix: Keep the clip in one place instead of moving it into show_llm_calls: give _llm_leaf an optional `suffix: str = ""` parameter appended AFTER the clip (`text = _truncate(text, clip)` then `text += suffix`), so the marker can never be clipped and the whitespace-normalization/clip logic stays single-sited. In show_llm_calls, stop appending the marker to body; instead compute it as the suffix: default view — `f"  (+{m['truncated'] - _LLM_PREVIEW_CHARS} chars · --full shows the stored text)"` (wording must not imply --full recovers everything — it only shows the 8k stored prefix); --full view — `f"  (stored record cut: +{m['truncated'] - len(m.get('content', ''))} chars not recorded)"` derived from the actual stored length (or import _LLM_MSG_CAP from stores.trace, which show_llm_calls already imports from), never a hard-coded 8000, so a future cap change can't drift. Consider routing the `[tool_calls: …]` append through the same suffix (it suffers the identical append-then-clip burial on long bodies today). Per CLAUDE.md's test rule, add a display test for show_llm_calls (e.g. in tests/test_core.py or a small test_trace_invoke.py): a >8000-char message renders the marker in default mode and the stored-cut disclosure in --full mode.

title: /trace why omits the human gate decisions — the causal chain skips the most consequential decisions in the run
description: _render_why (lines 728-807) renders plan, agent reasoning/tool choices, evidence, judge verdict, and cited sources — but never reads gate_events, which ride the approval node's event deltas in the same DB rows it already fetched. A 'decision provenance' view of a run where the user REJECTED a call therefore shows steps flagged skipped ('—' glyph) and an agent that mysteriously never got the data, with no line saying 'you declined run_shell, so the step was skipped'. The rail (live + replay) and /glass both render the human decision (_render_trust_annotations, gate_summary); why — the view explicitly pitched as the WHY — is the only trust surface that drops it, so the three surfaces tell inconsistent stories about the same run.
fix: In _render_why (commands/trace.py), after the 'how it reasoned' section, decode the already-fetched event deltas (same loop shape as _final_plan) collecting delta.get("gate_events"); when ANY exist, print a 'what you decided' block using the rail's exact vocabulary — '✓ you approved <names>' / '✗ you rejected <names>' with the '(quarantine escalation / tainted args)' suffix and the event's `step` label, so the '—' skipped plan steps above are explained.
