Two constraints the original fix missed: (a) per CLAUDE.md gotcha #7, when no gate_events appear in the deltas the block must be OMITTED entirely — never an affirmative 'no gates' / '0 gated' line, since for older recorded runs absence means unknown, not zero (the Glass Box's None-renders-nothing precedent); (b) don't re-roll the per-event flattening (approved names / rejected names / escalation suffix) — it already exists twice (tui/ui/trace.py::_render_trust_annotations and glassbox._gate_info); factor the event→summary step into one shared helper (e.g. on the gate_event record's home or glassbox) so the three surfaces can't drift, per the repo's one-home convention. Extend tests/test_gate_events.py (which already exercises _render_why directly at lines 181-201) with: a deltas-carrying-gate_events case asserting both the approved and rejected lines plus the step label, and a no-gate_events case asserting the block is absent.

title: Cloud-boundary egress ledger records the PRE-redaction payload size, not what was actually sent
description: _CloudBoundaryModel._outgoing (core/llms.py:66-73) computes `to_send, redactions = redaction.process_messages(messages)` and then sends `to_send` — but records `n_bytes=_approx_bytes(messages)`, the ORIGINAL un-redacted messages. In `runtime.redaction: redact` mode, redaction.process_messages returns copies with secrets replaced by placeholders (trust/redaction.py:131-156), so the bytes that actually cross the boundary differ from what the ledger records: the count includes the very secret characters that were stripped. The egress ledger is positioned as 'what ACTUALLY left this session' (/privacy egress, the per-answer trust receipt, the per-call ⇅ rail leaves, and the signed trust report all render this number), so the one mode whose whole point is changing the outbound payload is the mode where the recorded size is wrong. Char-count-vs-bytes is an acknowledged approximation; measuring the wrong object is not.
fix: Change core/llms.py:71 to `n_bytes=_approx_bytes(to_send)` as proposed. Test refinement: do not assert the count "shrinks" — a short secret (e.g. the email `a@b.co`) is replaced by the longer 16-char `[REDACTED:email]` placeholder, so the redacted payload can grow. Instead, in a new test (test_redaction.py or test_ollama_locality.py) drive _CloudBoundaryModel._outgoing with runtime.redaction=redact and a stub inner model, and assert the recorded event's n_bytes equals _approx_bytes of the messages the inner model actually received (or use a 40+ char sk-ant- key so the count strictly decreases).

title: @file mention dedup compares raw path strings, so the same file can be attached twice
description: find_mentions dedupes resolved paths by exact string (core/mentions.py:59-62: `if path and path not in seen`), and _resolve returns the path essentially as typed (only ~-expanded, core/mentions.py:43-52). Two spellings of the same file — `@a.txt` and `@./a.txt`, or a relative vs absolute form, or `@~/x` vs the expanded absolute — resolve to different strings, so expand() embeds the file's full contents twice in the attachments block (up to 2x _MAX_FILE_CHARS = 24k chars of duplicated context per file). The extra_paths drag-attach queue at core/mentions.py:112-115 has the same string comparison against the mention-resolved paths, so dragging a file already @-mentioned in the message double-attaches it too. Silent context-window waste plus a confusing doubled '#### label' section shown to the model.
fix: Dedupe on os.path.normcase(os.path.realpath(path)) instead of normcase(normpath(abspath(path))) — every candidate is already a verified-existing file, so realpath is safe, never raises, and additionally collapses Windows 8.3 short names (e.g. a dragged C:\Users\WORKST~1\... vs the long form, observed in repro) and symlink aliases that abspath/normpath miss. In find_mentions, key `seen` by that canonical form while storing the first-seen as-typed path as the value (keeps existing tests asserting found == [str(f)] passing and keeps display() labels as typed). In expand(), build the same canonical-key set from the mention-resolved paths and check each extra_path against it before appending. Extend tests/test_mentions.py with: (a) '@a.txt' + '@./a.txt' in one message asserting len(paths) == 1 and a single content occurrence in the block, and (b) expand('@a.txt', extra_paths=[absolute-path-to-a.txt]) asserting one attachment — use os.path.samefile for identity assertions as the existing trailing-punctuation test already does.

title: longest_overlap_many's greedy advance can skip the true longest span, contradicting its contract
description: After a match at position j, the scan advances `j += advanced` (the full matched length, line 319), skipping every start inside the matched region. A longer common span starting inside it is missed when `text` contains the seed in two places with different extents. Concrete (using min=2 for brevity; scales to _TAINT_MIN=40): a = 'ABCDEFGH Q CDEFGHIJKLMNOP', b = 'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP' -> j=0 matches 'ABCDEFGH' (8), jumps to j=8, finds 'IJKLMNOP' (8); the true longest 'CDEFGHIJKLMNOP' (14) at j=2 is never tried. The docstring promises 'The longest contiguous normalized span common to both texts'. `taint_scan` has the same pattern (`i = max(end, i+1)`, line 362) but acknowledges coarseness for adjacent sources. Impact is bounded: the binary tainted/not verdict is unaffected (some span >= _TAINT_MIN is still returned), but the Glass Box's displayed tainted_span and TaintHit.span_len — surfaces shown to the human as evidence — can understate or show the wrong (shorter) span.
fix: Prefer a different repair than the finding's "advance by 1": mirror _locate_span's backward extension inside longest_overlap_many's match loop — after the forward extension (x, y), also extend i0=i, j0=j backward while a[i0-1]==b[j0-1], take the candidate span b[j0:y], compare len(y-j0) against best, and advance j to y (the span END, same rationale as taint_scan's line 360-361 comment). This is zero-cost, and the empirical test proves it recovers the demonstrated class (taint_scan, which has backward extension, returned the full 70-char span on the same inputs). The finding's "advance by 1" alternative IS exact but has a quadratic hot case on the most common tainted scenario — an answer quoting a multi-KB source span makes every interior position hash-hit and forward-extend, O(L^2) per source in pure Python — so don't adopt it without that caveat. Since backward-extension + greedy advance is still not provably maximal in adversarial nested-repeat layouts, also soften the longest_overlap docstring ("a maximal common span; in adversarial repeat layouts not guaranteed to be THE longest") unless going exact. Add the two-occurrence regression case to tests/test_glassbox.py for longest_overlap/longest_overlap_many specifically — not taint_scan, which already handles it (line 362 needs no change; the finding's claim that TaintHit.span_len shares the bug is incorrect for this construction).

title: permissions.json written non-atomically and read with a silent empty-dict fallback — corruption silently drops user-RAISED risk overrides
description: `_save` uses a plain `path.write_text` (line 138) — a crash/power-loss mid-write leaves a truncated JSON — and `_load` (lines 122-132) swallows OSError/ValueError into `data = {}` with no diag.log or user warning (diag isn't even imported here). The gate then runs with empty `risk_overrides` and `shell_allow`. Lost /allow prefixes fail safe, but a lost override that RAISED a tool's tier (e.g. `/risk web_search destructive --save`) silently weakens the gate below the posture the operator believes is in force — the exact failure mode `apply_profile`'s own docstring (lines 240-244) treats as a hard error on the import path. For the file that IS the durable gate policy, a corruption must be loud, not a silent reset.
fix: In trust/policy.py: (1) make `_save` atomic by mirroring stores/memory_registry._atomic_write — dump to a sibling `permissions.json.tmp` then `os.replace` (atomic on Windows + POSIX); keep it a small local helper since policy.py is a leaf that must not import stores. (2) In `_load`, distinguish `FileNotFoundError` (normal first run — stay silent, current behavior) from other OSError / json.JSONDecodeError / non-dict payloads (corruption): keep the degrade-to-defaults (test_policy.py::test_corrupt_policy_file_fails_safe requires never raising into the gate) but record the failure — import `diag` (a no-project-imports leaf already used by sibling trust leaf signing.py; update policy.py's "Imports only config" docstring and CLAUDE.md's key-files entry in the same change) and diag.log the path + error, and stash it in a module-level accessor, e.g. `policy.load_problem() -> str|None`. (3) Surface the user warning from agent.main at startup via the existing mcp_client.problems() pattern (one ui.warn line when `load_problem()` is set) — NOT from registry.py via ui, which would have tools/ import tui/ against the layering rule. (4) Because any mutation after a corrupt load overwrites the on-disk file with defaults-plus-one-entry, fire the diag/problem record at first load, and optionally rename the unreadable file to permissions.json.corrupt before the first _save so the prior posture stays recoverable. Extend tests/test_policy.py in the same change: corrupt file → load_problem() set / diag called; FileNotFoundError → no problem recorded; _save leaves no .tmp residue and survives an existing target on Windows (os.replace).

title: Posture line and signed trust report attest the raw runtime.quarantine string instead of the effective mode
description: `posture_spans` reads `q = str(cfg.get("runtime.quarantine", "gate"))` (receipt.py:183) and `build_report` does the same into the SIGNED body (trust_report.py:95), while the operating mode is `quarantine.mode()` (quarantine.py:110-113), which lowercases and falls back to 'gate' for any invalid value. With `runtime.quarantine: none` (or 'OFF', 'Warn', etc.) the banner posture line and the portable signed attestation state a mode that is not the one in force — e.g. posture renders 'quarantine none' as a calm dim fact and styles warn only on the exact string 'off', and the report attests `boundary.quarantine: "none"` while the system actually runs 'gate'. Both surfaces explicitly promise the LIVE posture ('this line must never claim a posture it didn't read'; 'here is exactly how this agent was allowed to behave').
fix: Route ALL posture/attestation surfaces through quarantine.mode(), not just the two cited sites: (1) trust/receipt.py:188 — `q = quarantine.mode()` (lazy import inside the existing try block; quarantine is a leaf importing only config+textutil, no cycle), keeping the warn style keyed on the normalized "off"; (2) trust/trust_report.py:107 — `"quarantine": quarantine.mode()` (mirrors the adjacent `redaction.mode()` at line 106; no artifact-format bump — value normalization, not a schema change); (3) commands/privacy.py:177, commands/policy.py:337, and commands/policy.py:481 — the bare /privacy and /policy posture readouts have the identical raw read, and fixing only receipt/report would leave the posture line saying "gate" while /policy says "none", a new cross-surface drift (commands/privacy.py:607 self-corrects once build_report is fixed, since it renders the report dict). Extend tests/test_ambient_trust.py and tests/test_trust_report.py with an invalid config value (e.g. "none") and a case variant ("OFF") asserting the effective mode renders/attests — including that "OFF" gets the warn style.

title: -o output-flag guard has drifted: /policy export and /privacy report accept flag-shaped paths (and /policy export swallows stray flags as a destination)
description: `/trace export` refuses both a dangling AND a flag-shaped `-o` value (commands/trace.py:392 `if out_path is None or out_path.startswith("-")`), and its comment claims "Same guard as /privacy report and /policy export". Neither sibling implements the second half: `/privacy report -o -o` (commands/privacy.py:589-595) happily writes a report to a file literally named `-o`, and `/policy export -o --out` does the same (commands/policy.py:364-369 checks only None). Worse, `/policy export` treats EVERY unrecognized token as a positional destination (`dest_str = " ".join(positional)`, line 370-371), so `/policy export --save` — a natural habit, since `--save` is the universal persist flag everywhere else — silently writes the profile to a cwd file named `--save` instead of refusing. Three hand-rolled copies of the same iterator parse are exactly how the guard drifted.
fix: Extract `split_out_flag(args) -> (rest, path|None, error|None)` into commands/_utils.py implementing the -o/--out/--output synonyms with refusal of a dangling OR flag-shaped value (the trace.py:392 semantics), and route /trace export, /privacy report, and /policy export through it. Two corrections to the proposal: (a) in /policy export, a flag-shaped POSITIONAL (e.g. `--save`) should REFUSE with usage + "nothing written" rather than warn-and-continue — /trace export's warn-and-continue exports to the default path, which is exactly the "silently write the default" failure the guard's own rationale forbids; a user typing `/policy export --save` made a grammar error, not a request for the default destination (a non-flag positional path stays a valid destination — test_policy_export_positional_still_works pins it). (b) Per CLAUDE.md's test rule, extend the suite in the same change: flag-shaped `-o` refusal tests for /policy export and /privacy report (mirroring test_replay_and_source.py:170-174) plus a `/policy export --save` writes-nothing test; keep the existing dangling tests green, and update the now-truthful comment in trace.py and the docstring claims at tests/test_policy_commands.py:5-8.

title: /rewind's "N earlier turn(s) remain" counts compaction summaries and steer notes as turns
description: `drop_last_turn` defines a turn boundary as a HumanMessage that is NOT a steer note and NOT a compaction summary (commands/conversation.py:159-163), but `_rewind`'s confirmation line counts every message whose class name is HumanMessage (lines 223-225) — and both the compaction summary (core/compaction.py, a tagged HumanMessage) and the standalone steer note are HumanMessages. Concretely: after `/compact` (or auto-compaction) the history is [summary HumanMessage, …most-recent turn]; `/rewind` drops the turn and prints "1 earlier turn(s) remain; /rewind again to keep walking back", then the very next `/rewind` contradicts it with "nothing to rewind — the conversation is empty" (human_idxs filters the summary out). The two readers of the same list disagree about what a turn is.
fix: In _rewind (commands/conversation.py:223-225), count with the identical filter drop_last_turn uses, adding the same local imports the module's other helpers use (HumanMessage, is_summary, is_steer_message are imported locally in drop_last_turn/_last_query, not at module level): `remaining = sum(1 for m in ctx.state.get("messages", []) if isinstance(m, HumanMessage) and not is_steer_message(m) and not is_summary(m))`. This also fixes the off-by-N case (N real turns + summary previously reported N+1). With remaining == 0 the existing "the conversation is now empty" line now agrees with the next /rewind's "nothing to rewind" output, which is the contract. Add a test in tests/test_rewind.py (or a new _rewind-level test) asserting that after rewinding the last real turn with only a summary left, the remain/empty messaging matches what a subsequent /rewind reports.

title: /clear leaves the autosave slot holding the cleared conversation — /resume resurrects it after quit
description: `_session.write_autosave` deliberately no-ops on an empty message list, and its docstring states the contract: "a caller that has deliberately emptied the conversation uses clear_autosave() instead" (commands/_session.py:83-94). `/rewind` honors it when it empties the conversation (conversation.py:189-192); `/clear` (conversation.py:52-67) does not — it resets state and never touches the slot. So `/clear` → `/quit` (write_autosave skips the now-empty state) → relaunch → `/resume` restores exactly the conversation the user explicitly cleared. The /clear help enumerates what is NOT touched (config, corpus, memory, trace) and the autosave isn't on that list, so a reader infers it's dropped. For a privacy-positioned product, the "fresh start" command silently keeping the old conversation restorable is a surprising inconsistency between the two deliberately-empty paths.
fix: In _clear (commands/conversation.py), clear the slot only when a non-empty conversation was actually discarded, mirroring drop_last_turn's semantics: before `ctx.state = ctx.make_initial_state()`, capture `had_messages = bool(ctx.state.get("messages"))`; after the reset, `if had_messages: clear_autosave()`. (An unconditional clear_autosave() would wipe the previous session's autosave when /clear is typed at a fresh launch with no conversation — the exact case write_autosave's empty-guard protects.) Also add the autosave slot to /clear's details (it IS dropped) and extend tests/test_sessions.py or a new test to pin both branches: /clear with messages removes _autosave.json; /clear with an already-empty conversation leaves a pre-existing autosave intact.

title: /config reload is the only case-sensitive subcommand match
description: Every other `/config` subcommand matches case-insensitively — `key`/`keys` (line 189), `doctor/setup/check/health` (193), `persist` (197) all compare `args[0].lower()` — but line 223 is `if args[0] == "reload":` exact-case. `/config Reload` (or RELOAD) therefore falls through to the dotted-key reader and prints the baffling `Reload = None` instead of reloading config.yaml, breaking the module's own grammar consistency.
fix: Change the comparison to `args[0].lower() == "reload"` to match the sibling branches (one-line fix, no behavior change for the lowercase spelling).

title: sync() rewrites the entire vector-store dump and index on every startup even when nothing changed
description: `store.dump(str(_store_path()))` + `_write_index(...)` (rag.py:442-443) run unconditionally at the end of every sync — including the common startup case of zero added/updated/removed files (the case the docstring optimizes for: "a startup whose corpus hasn't changed does zero embedding calls"). InMemoryVectorStore.dump serializes every vector to JSON, so a non-trivial corpus rewrites a multi-MB vectors.json on every launch for no reason; it also widens the crash window in which a kill mid-write leaves a truncated dump, which `_load_store` then treats as corrupt and silently forces a full re-embed of the whole corpus. (Distinct from the deferred rag.sync non-transactionality item, which is about manifest-vs-dump ordering, and from the deferred `_file_hash` re-read item.)
fix: Skip the dump and index rewrite when nothing mutated the store: `if full_rebuild or to_embed or stats["removed"]: store.dump(...); _write_index(...)` — behavior-preserving since an unchanged store/index round-trips to identical content.

title: Tracer.log_event / start_run / end_run are not best-effort: a sqlite failure in the observability layer kills the live turn
description: `log_llm_call` (trace.py:138-153) is explicitly wrapped — "a logging failure must never propagate into the running model call" — but `log_event` (line 129), `start_run` (119), and `end_run` (160) have no guard. `log_event` is called from `run_turn`'s on_update hook for every node delta (agent.py:197), so a transient sqlite error (database/db.sqlite locked by a second Saturn instance or an open DB browser — it's shared with SqliteSaver — or disk full) raises out of the stream loop and lands in the turn's `except Exception` (agent.py:981-989): the turn is reported failed and the answer lost even though the agent pipeline itself was healthy. Worse, `end_run` inside that same except handler (agent.py:988) would re-raise and crash the REPL loop. The watcher should never take down the watched.
fix: Apply the log_llm_call try/except-diag.log pattern to log_event, start_run (return -1 on failure; log_event/end_run with run_id -1 are harmless orphan writes/no-op updates, and headless --export already fails loudly via its own guarded path), and end_run — but add a one-shot circuit breaker: on the first write failure, set a self._broken flag (diag.log once) and make subsequent writes no-ops for the rest of the run/session, because each failed execute/commit otherwise blocks up to sqlite's 5s default busy timeout PER node delta, turning a dead observability layer into a multi-second stall on every update. Optionally reset the flag in start_run so the next turn retries once. Keep the seq increments so any later successful rows stay ordered. The diag-import hoist is safe (diag.py has zero project imports) but cosmetic. Do not add a ui.warn from trace.py (stores/ must not import tui); if a user-visible "trace recording degraded" notice is wanted, expose a Tracer.broken property and warn from agent.py's loop. Extend tests/test_core.py with a failure-injection test (e.g. monkeypatched conn.execute raising) asserting log_event/start_run/end_run swallow and that the sentinel run_id round-trips.

title: add_memory writes a multi-line fact verbatim, silently breaking the one-fact-per-bullet contract and losing data on remove_memory
description: `remember(fact)` (tools/knowledge.py:46) passes the model-supplied string straight to `add_memory`, which embeds it verbatim into `f"- ({date.today()}) {tag}{fact}\n"` (memory_registry.py:84). A fact containing a newline (models routinely emit multi-line strings) writes a bullet whose continuation lines are not bullets, so: `_facts()` (line 49-55) returns only the first line — `read_memory_block` injects a truncated fact into every turn's grounding context; `/memory` numbering counts only first lines; and `remove_memory` (line 111-122) rewrites the file as `_HEADER + bullets`, permanently discarding every continuation line of every other stored fact on the first removal. The dedup check (line 77) also misses, since it compares against the first line only. Same hole for `category` containing `]` or a newline (corrupts the `_PREFIX_RE` prefix parse).
fix: The proposed fix is the right shape and location; tighten it as follows. In add_memory (stores/memory_registry.py), normalize at the top, BEFORE the empty check and the dedup comparison so dedup compares the sanitized form: `fact = " ".join((fact or "").split())` (collapses newlines/tabs/runs of spaces to single spaces, matching the file's one-fact-per-bullet contract). Sanitize category the same way plus strip the prefix-breaking bracket: `category = " ".join((category or "").split()).replace("]", "")` — falling back to "general" if it sanitizes to empty (only `]` and whitespace break the `_PREFIX_RE` parse; `[` is harmless to `[^\]]*`). Do this in add_memory, not the `remember` tool, so the /memory add command path and any future caller share the one write boundary. Per CLAUDE.md's rule (change a load-bearing surface → extend the suite in the same change), add a small regression test (e.g. tests/test_memory_registry.py): multi-line fact round-trips as one bullet readable in full by list_memory/read_memory_block; remove_memory of an unrelated fact preserves the others byte-complete; duplicate multi-line fact dedups; category containing `]`/newline still strips cleanly via _fact_text.

title: `saturn --json` without -p is silently ignored and launches the interactive TUI
description: _parse_cli (agent.py:426-439) enforces cross-flag rules for `-p ""`, `-p`+`--replay`, and `--export`-without-`-p` ("a typo'd invocation must never silently fall through to the interactive TUI"), but has no rule for `--json` without `-p`. `saturn --json` (and `saturn --json --replay file`) parses fine, `_args.json` is only consulted inside the headless branch (agent.py:705), so the flag is silently dropped and the interactive TUI launches -- exactly the silent fallthrough the strict-CLI contract exists to prevent, and inconsistent with the `--export` rule one line above. A script author who forgets `-p` gets a hung interactive session instead of exit 2. No test asserts the current behavior (tests/test_cli.py covers the other three rules only), so the fix breaks no contract.
fix: Add to _parse_cli: `if args.json and args.prompt is None: parser.error("--json only applies to a headless turn -- use it with -p/--prompt")`, mirroring the --export rule, and extend tests/test_cli.py alongside test_export_requires_prompt.

title: A configured session token budget silently corrupts trust-benchmark grades into spurious FAILs
description: core/budget.py keeps one process-wide `_spent` total fed by every agent/synthesize call, and `route_after_agent` force-lands turns with no new tool rounds once `exceeded()` (runtime.token_budget > 0). The benchmark runs dozens of queries in one process and never resets or records this: if the user has set `runtime.token_budget` in config.yaml (the knob exists exactly for the cloud tiers the benchmark also exercises), spend accumulates across queries and once the ceiling is crossed every later grounding bait completes "ok" but with no web_search and no replan -- graded "ungrounded" (run_trust_benchmark, benchmark.py:163-168), a FAIL that exits 1 under --strict -- while gate probes execute no gated calls, making coverage vacuous. The grader explicitly guards the analogous posture problem for the gate (`policy_default` is recorded and an elevated policy is "reported, not failed", benchmark.py:199/229), but the budget posture is neither neutralized nor recorded, so the headline trust artifact silently misreports confabulation that never happened.
fix: Drop the budget.reset() option: the knob is a deliberate user cost ceiling for cloud tiers, and the benchmark silently zeroing it per-query would let a benchmark run spend unboundedly past a cap the user set precisely to bound real cloud cost — the opposite of the product's trust posture. Instead mirror the existing gate-policy treatment exactly (reported, not failed): in run_trust_benchmark, record the posture in the summary (e.g. summary["grounding"]["token_budget"] = budget.limit()); capture budget.exceeded() BEFORE each grounding bait runs and, for any entry that would grade "ungrounded" while the budget was already spent (or crossed during that turn), assign a distinct verdict like "budget_capped" excluded from the "ungrounded" count; leave trust_failures unchanged (it keys off the ungrounded count, so budget_capped entries no longer fail strict runs); and print a one-line note alongside the existing elevated-policy line ("token_budget spent at query N — later grounding grades not meaningful") plus the same note in the gate section when probes ran with 0 gated calls under an exceeded budget. Extend tests/test_cli.py's trust_failures decision test with a budget_capped summary, matching how the elevated-policy case is locked in.

title: /trace invoke truncation marker is unreachable — `--full` presents storage-truncated LLM messages as complete
description: `if m.get('truncated'): body += f"  (+{m['truncated'] - _LLM_PREVIEW_CHARS} chars)" if not full else ""` is dead in both branches, verified empirically. stores/trace.py sets `truncated` = the ORIGINAL content length only when content exceeds _LLM_MSG_CAP (8000), storing an 8000-char slice. In preview mode the marker is appended to the END of that >=8000-char body and then `_llm_leaf` clips the whole string to 240 chars (`_truncate(text, clip)` at trace.py:474-475), cutting the marker off every time. In `--full` mode the marker is explicitly suppressed (`else ""`), so `/trace invoke --full` — whose docstring promises 'the entire stored message text' — renders a recording-truncated message with zero indication it was cut (the stored slice has no ellipsis). The arithmetic is also wrong: it subtracts the display clip (240) rather than the storage cap (8000). For a product whose pitch is honest observability, the --full case silently overstates fidelity.
fix: In show_llm_calls (tui/ui/trace.py), compute the recording-truncation delta as `m["truncated"] - len(m.get("content", ""))` instead of importing the private _LLM_MSG_CAP from stores.trace — it is correct by construction even if the storage cap changes between recording and rendering, and avoids coupling to a private constant. Render it in BOTH modes as a suffix that survives clipping: pass it to _llm_leaf as a separate `note: str | None` parameter appended AFTER `_truncate(text, clip)` (dim style), e.g. `(+N chars truncated at recording)` — in preview mode the existing trailing "…" already marks the display clip, so the note should state recording truncation specifically, not `truncated - 240`. Scope note: the output message (_output_to_dict, stores/trace.py:223) is also capped at 8000 but never records a `truncated` key, so the `out` leaf cannot be flagged without a small storage-side addition — flag it there too (same `truncated = len(text)` convention) or document the gap. Add a display-side test next to test_core.py:411 covering both modes (marker visible in preview, "+N chars truncated at recording" visible under --full).

title: Plan-review '← current' marker points at the wrong step after a structural edit
description: review_plan captures `active_id` once from the interrupt payload (plan.py:191) and re-renders with it after every edit (plan.py:225). But plan_ops `add_step`/`drop_step`/`move_step` renumber step_ids 1..N (`_renumber`), so after any structural edit `_render_review_plan`'s comparison `step.get('step_id') == active_id` (plan.py:122) highlights whichever step now wears the OLD number, not the actually-active step. Example: active step #2, user types `add "x" at 1` — the old step #1 becomes #2 and gets the '← current' marker; the real resume point (now #3) is unmarked. The user is making a human-control decision (run/abort/edit) against a wrong claim about where execution resumes. Statuses are preserved across edits, so the truth is still in the plan.
fix: In review_plan (tui/ui/plan.py), stop carrying the payload's active_id across edits. Recompute the marker target before each _render_review_plan call as the first non-terminal step of the CURRENT plan — `from core.state import active_step` (lazy import, matching the existing lazy `from core import plan_ops`), then `active_id = (active_step(plan) or {}).get("step_id")` — i.e., the exact function plan_gate used to build the payload and that agent lockstep will use on the edited plan at resume, so the marker can never disagree with resume semantics. This also fixes the drift the original finding missed: `status <id> done|skipped` edits that move the resume point without any renumbering. Render no marker when the plan is complete (active_step returns None). Do NOT key on status=='active' — at the first gate every step is 'pending' ('active' is only assigned by update_plan_node after a tool round), and a payload-id fallback would reintroduce the stale marker there.

title: Turn-boundary predicate is hand-rolled in five places — the drift already produced one live bug
description: The predicate 'this HumanMessage starts a real turn' (`isinstance(m, HumanMessage) and not is_steer_message(m) and not is_summary(m)`) is independently re-spelled at agent.py:236-240, commands/conversation.py:162, commands/conversation.py:290, nodes/ground.py:79 (inverted continue-form), and core/compaction.py:78-80 — where the fifth copy drifted (missing the steer check, the high-severity bug reported above). state.py:13-17 documents the invariant in prose, but nothing enforces it structurally; every future slicer re-rolls the same three-clause filter. This is distinct from the deferred positional-multiset-walker triplication (deferred-review #2) — these five sites ARE byte-identical in intent and trivially extractable.
fix: Add `is_turn_start(m) -> bool` in core/state.py next to is_steer_message (lazy `from core.compaction import is_summary` inside the function — no cycle exists today since compaction.py never imports core.state, but lazy keeps core.state import-light and matches the existing lazy-import style at all five call sites; alternatively move the _SUMMARY_PREFIX startswith test into state.py). Replace SIX occurrences, not five: agent.py:236-240, commands/conversation.py:159-163, commands/conversation.py:290, nodes/ground.py:79 (use `if not is_turn_start(m): continue` inside the HumanMessage branch), AND BOTH compaction.py sites — lines 78-81 (boundary computation) and 99-101 (summarized_turns stats). Note the two compaction replacements are a behavior CHANGE (they add the missing steer check — the companion bug fix), so add a new test pinning steer-note-is-not-a-boundary for summarize_messages, mirroring tests/test_compact_history.py:50 (existing tests pin the other four sites but not this one). Finally, update the state.py:13-16 prose comment to name the new predicate and add compaction.summarize_messages to its consumer list, so the doc and the structure agree.

title: llms.capability_of(role) is dead code
description: core/llms.py:181-184 defines `capability_of(role)` but nothing in the repo calls it: a repo-wide search for `capability_of` finds only config.py:163 (the Config method definition), config.py:216 and llms.py:491 (both calling `cfg.capability_of(model)` directly), and this dead wrapper. check_models, doctor v2, and the commands all go through the Config method with a concrete model id. Verified against the current on-disk version of the file (which carries the in-flight remote-Ollama changes).
fix: Delete the four-line function. No test references it (grep over tests/ finds no use), so removal is behavior-preserving.

title: Fold the two duplicated gathered-material section blocks in synthesize_node
description: synthesize_node builds the 'Tool results' and 'Retrieved documents' prompt sections with two structurally identical ~11-line blocks (synthesize.py:120-141): each picks numbered vs plain body, picks a citations vs plain header, and appends one HumanMessage. The only differences are the item list, the numbered list, and the two header strings. This is genuine local duplication — a third gathered-material section (or a header tweak) currently has to be edited in two places that must stay parallel with build_sources' numbering order.
fix:
In nodes/synthesize.py: (1) initialize `numbered_tools: list[str] = []` and `numbered_docs: list[str] = []` next to `sources` at line 109 so the names are bound when runtime.citations is false; (2) extract a helper keyed on the section NAME, not four header strings — e.g. `def _gathered_section(items, numbered, citations, name): if not items: return None; if citations: return HumanMessage(content=f"{name} (numbered — cite the matching [n] after claims drawn from them):
" + "

".join(numbered)); return HumanMessage(content=f"{name}:
" + "

".join(map(str, items)))` — the suffixes are already byte-identical across both sections, so this reconstructs the exact current headers while folding the shared citation-instruction sentence to one occurrence; (3) call it twice in the existing order (tool_results with "Tool results", then documents_retrieved with "Retrieved documents", keeping the pre-formatted-strings comment above the second call) and append non-None results, preserving build_sources' tools-then-docs numbering order. No test changes needed; optionally add a small unit test pinning the two header strings to guard the prompt bytes.

title: Identical _host() hostname helper duplicated in web.py and mcp_client.py
description: web.py _host (lines 75-80) and mcp_client.py _host (lines 380-387) are byte-equivalent: 'try: return urlparse(url).hostname or str(url) except Exception: return str(url)'. Both feed the egress ledger, so a single source for 'how a host label is derived for egress' is also correctness-aligned (egress.py has a third near-identical urlparse-hostname block at lines 102-108). Genuine duplication, behavior-preserving to fold.
fix: Add a public helper to trust/egress.py (e.g., `def host_of(url: str) -> str` next to `_host_label`, moving `from urllib.parse import urlparse` to module top), with the exact existing logic: `try: return urlparse(url).hostname or str(url) / except Exception: return str(url)`. Replace the local `_host` in tools/web.py and tools/mcp_client.py with `egress.host_of(...)` — both modules already import `trust.egress`, so no new imports and mcp_client's "imports only leaf modules" constraint is untouched. Do NOT fold egress.ollama_is_local()'s urlparse block into the helper: its fallback semantics deliberately differ (empty-string/False, fails toward NOT local), and substituting host_of's raw-URL fallback would change failure behavior of the local-inference attestation. Do not place the helper in textutil.py — its documented scope is text-shaping idioms, and egress.py is the documented single chokepoint for egress host labeling.

title: The scaffold machinery in _framework.py is dead code
description: No command anywhere registers `implemented=False` (grep: the flag is referenced only inside _framework.py itself), so the entire scaffold pathway is unreachable: `_todo()` (lines 100-106), the `implemented` dataclass field (44) and decorator parameter (58), the "(scaffold)" suffix in `command_completions` (89), the "(scaffolded — prints intended behaviour only…)" line in `_show_help` (186-187), and the dispatch guard (230-232). It is the leftover plumbing of the removed "* = scaffolded" era — tests/test_help.py:56 only asserts the legend stays gone, nothing exercises the flag.
fix: Delete the `implemented` field/parameter and the four dead branches (~20 LOC across _framework.py). Behavior-preserving: every live command passes the default True, and no test or caller reads the attribute.

title: Framed byte-faithful wrap loop is triplicated across the shell/http/full-args renderers
description: The identical block — header line, `width = max(20, _term_width() - 12)`, then `for chunk in _wrap_exact(line, width): row = Text(); row.append('  ┃ ', style='bold'); row.append(prefix, style=_DIM); row.append(chunk, style='default'); _console.print(row)` plus its plain-print twin — appears three times: _render_shell_command (approval.py:218-236, prefix '$ '), _render_http_request (approval.py:262-280, prefix '> '), and _render_full_args (approval.py:318-336, prefix ''). Six copies counting the plain branches, ~70 LOC of which ~35 fold away. This is exactly the codebase's own 'one home for the idiom' rule (cf. textutil.head_tail): the byte-faithful-wrap safety contract these comments each restate should live once.
fix: Extract ONLY the wrapped-body loop into one helper in tui/ui/approval.py, leaving headers in the callers (the header shapes genuinely differ: rich "↳ command"/"↳ request" vs plain "-> command"/"-> request" for shell/http, and per-key "{k} =" with no arrow in _render_full_args). E.g.: `def _frame_wrapped(lines, prefix, width=None): width = width if width is not None else max(20, _term_width() - 12);` rich branch does the for-line/for-chunk `_wrap_exact` loop printing `"  ┃ "` bold + f"      {prefix}" dim + chunk default; plain branch prints f"  ┃       {prefix}{line}" UNWRAPPED (preserving the existing rich-wraps/plain-doesn't asymmetry). Move the byte-faithful-contract comment into the helper's docstring (one home for the idiom, cf. _wrap_exact/_clamp_value). Callers: _render_shell_command prints its head + `_frame_wrapped(lines, "$ ")` + keeps the tip row in the caller (the tip is NOT _wrap_exact-wrapped today — routing it through the helper would change output at narrow widths); _render_http_request prints its head + `_frame_wrapped(lines, "> ")`; _render_full_args computes width once before the per-key loop and passes it (`_frame_wrapped(lines, "", width=width)`) after printing each `{k} =` head, preserving the loop-invariant single _term_width() read the file's own comments call out. Keep _wrap_exact, _full_width_args, and _clamp_value untouched so tests/test_gate_ux.py passes unchanged; do NOT fold _render_diff_rows or _frame_note in (they truncate/textwrap deliberately — a different contract).

title: ask_approval duplicates the entire per-call gating logic across its rich and plain branches
description: The per-call loop body — the is_write/is_edit/is_shell/is_http flags, the bespoke-renderer skip-key conditions (content / old_string+new_string / command / url+method+headers+body), the `_full_width_args` dispatch, the four bespoke-renderer calls, `_render_secret_warnings`, `_render_taint_warning`, and the risk hint — is written twice nearly verbatim (approval.py:587-638 rich, 653-687 plain); only the head line and the compact-arg row rendering differ. Today adding a bespoke-rendered tool requires three coordinated edits (both loops + _BESPOKE_RENDERED), and the skip-key lists can silently drift between the two branches — in a file where the rendering IS the safety surface. ~35 LOC fold.
fix: As proposed, with one tightening: don't put a {tool: skip_keys} table NEXT TO _BESPOKE_RENDERED (that just creates a new two-place drift between the table and the tuple). Make one table the single source — e.g. _BESPOKE = {"write_file": (("content",), _render_write_diff), "edit_file": (("old_string", "new_string"), _render_edit_diff), "run_shell": (("command",), _render_shell_command), "http_request": (("url", "method", "headers", "body"), _render_http_request)} — and derive _BESPOKE_RENDERED = tuple(_BESPOKE) from it so _full_width_args (and test_gate_ux's membership assertions) keep working unchanged. Extract the shared loop body into _render_call(tc): risk/head line and compact-arg row and risk-hint branch on _RICH internally (matching the established sub-renderer pattern); skip keys and the bespoke renderer call come from the one table; hoist arg_repr (pure: _truncate(repr(v), 80)) to module scope. Output stays byte-identical; adding a future bespoke-rendered tool becomes exactly one table entry.

title: /resume save accepts reserved-verb names that the loader can then never reach
description: `_save_named` (conversation.py:407-422) accepts any name, but `_resume`'s router (380-390) intercepts `save/--save/-s`, the LIST_VERBS (`list/ls/--list/-l`), every REMOVE_VERB (`remove/rm/delete/del/forget/drop`), and `rename/mv/--rename` BEFORE the load-by-name branch. So `/resume save list` succeeds and creates list.json, but `/resume list` thereafter shows the listing — no documented spelling loads that session. (`/resume "list"` happens to work only because `safe_stem` strips the quote characters — an accident of the sanitizer, not grammar.) `/policy allow` fixed this exact reserved-word trap with an explicit `add` escape hatch and a resolve-before-removing rule; /resume's load path has neither. Same applies to a session named `rm`, `drop`, `rename`, etc.
fix: Refuse reserved stems at creation time in BOTH creation sites, mirroring /policy allow's lone-verb reservation: after computing path = _session_file(name), reject when path.stem.lower() is in the router-reserved set — ("save",) + LIST_VERBS + REMOVE_VERBS + ("rename", "mv") (flag-shaped tokens like "-s"/"--list" need no reservation: safe_stem sanitizes them to non-colliding stems). Apply it in _save_named ("'list' is a /resume subcommand — pick another name") AND in _rename_named's destination (/resume rename notes list strands the session identically; the dest.exists() check there is the natural place). Compare against the SANITIZED stem case-insensitively, since the router lowercases args[0] (/resume save LIST strands too) and the stem is what the save confirmation tells the user to retype. Keep load/delete/rename resolution unchanged so pre-existing colliding files stay reachable. Extend tests/test_command_grammar.py with a parametrized refusal test over the reserved set for both save and rename.

title: Plain-fallback banner ignores the redesign's 2-space indent rule
description: The uncommitted redesign's stated contract (new tui/ui/__init__.py docstring: 'everything sits on a 2-space indent') holds in the rich banner (`Text("  ")`, prompt.py:97/105) but the plain fallback prints both banner lines at column 0 (`print(f"saturday.ai  {model} · ctx {ctx}")` / `print(f"{n_tools} tools · ...")`, prompt.py:115-116), while the posture_line plain fallback directly beneath it IS indented (prompt.py:155: `print("  " + ...)`). On the no-rich path the session header therefore sits flush-left above an indented posture line and indented everything-else — a visible misalignment on the exact surface the redesign is polishing.
fix: Prefix both plain banner lines with the same two spaces as the rich path: `print(f"  saturday.ai  {model} · ctx {ctx}")` and `print(f"  {n_tools} tools · {n_docs} docs{git} · {cwd} · /help")`.

title: Plan-review frame: the active step's row overflows the terminal and wraps, breaking the ┃ gutter on exactly the row being reviewed
description: `_plan_line_bare` budgets the label as `_term_width() - 14 - len(tag)` (line 31), sized for the 4-char trace rail. The review frame instead prepends `"  ┃ "` (4 chars, `_render_review_plan` line 126) AND appends the 10-char `"  ← current"` marker (line 124) to the active row. Net: at width 100 with a label at the cap, the active row is ~108 cells, so it soft-wraps with the continuation at column 0 — the heavy frame's gutter is broken, and the `← current` marker (the whole point of the row) lands stranded on an unindented wrap line. Planner labels are full sentences, so hitting the truncation cap is the common case, not the corner.
fix: Keep the proposed mechanism but fix the arithmetic. Add `reserve: int = 0` to `_plan_line_bare` and fold it into the budget: `label = _truncate(str(step.get("label", "")), max(20, tw - 14 - len(tag) - reserve))`. In `_render_review_plan`, pass `reserve=11` — the exact cell width of the "  ← current" marker; the frame prefix "  ┃ " is 4 cells, identical to the rail already covered by the 14-budget, so there is no frame delta (the finding's "frame delta 2 + marker 10 = 12" is a double miscount that happens to land 1 cell over). Apply the reserve to every review row, not just the active one, so the truncation column stays uniform across the frame. Live-rail callers (`_plan_line`, hence render_plan/show_plan) pass nothing — byte-identical output there. Extend tests/test_gate_ux.py with a width assertion: monkeypatch the module's `_term_width` to a fixed width, build a step with an over-cap label plus an intended_tool, assemble the review row exactly as _render_review_plan does (frame prefix + bare row + marker), and assert its cell_len (or len of the plain-fallback string) <= the forced width.

title: /trace replay's recorded final answer prints unwrapped — long paragraphs wrap to column 0, breaking the 2-space indent rhythm
description: show_run renders the recorded answer line-by-line as `Text("  ") + line` (lines 449-460) with no width handling. Model answers are typically long single-line paragraphs, so the terminal soft-wraps them with continuations at column 0 — the only block in the whole replay that escapes the app's 2-space indent (the __init__ docstring: "everything sits on a 2-space indent"). Every neighboring renderer in this file hanging-indents (`_emit_result_leaf`, `_emit_message_leaf`, `_node_leaf`); the deliberately quiet recorded-answer block ends up the visually messiest part of the drill-down.
fix: In the rich path, render the recorded answer body through the codebase's existing "plaintext answer body" idiom (response.py:257, _print_markdown_body's fallback): `_console.print(Padding(Text(response_text, style=_DIM), (0, 0, 0, 2)), width=min(_term_width(), 100))` (add `from rich.padding import Padding` to trace.py). Padding keeps every wrapped continuation line on the 2-space indent, rich's wrap preserves intra-line leading whitespace (code blocks / nested lists in the recorded answer keep their shape — naive textwrap.wrap with default drop_whitespace=True would strip it), and the ~100-col measure matches the live answer's _BODY_WIDTH. Keep the faint `╶ final answer (recorded)` label and _DIM style unchanged. For the no-rich fallback path only, wrap per line with `textwrap.wrap(ln, max(20, _term_width() - 4), replace_whitespace=False, drop_whitespace=False) or [""]`, printing each piece behind the 2-space indent (blank lines preserved as [""]).

title: /context and /models trailing hint/binding lines render unstyled (bright default), inverting the dim-hint hierarchy
description: show_context emits its `roles: …` line and the `set with /context <size> …` usage hint via bare `_emit(f"  …")` (lines 78-81), and show_models emits its `bindings:` block the same way (lines 142-145). `_emit` prints plain strings, which rich renders in the default (bright) style — so the most incidental content on the screen (a usage hint) renders BRIGHTER than the dim-labeled data rows and gauges above it, the opposite of the stated hierarchy ("metrics are tertiary — dim"; hints elsewhere are uniformly dim: section subtitles, the receipt tail, `note()`). The bindings block also hand-rolls indentation instead of the `ui.table` vocabulary CLAUDE.md mandates for listings, so role/model columns don't align with the rail like every other readout.
fix: In tui/ui/readouts.py, dim the incidental lines using the existing trace.py:522 idiom so the no-rich fallback stays byte-identical (Text is None without rich — never construct it unguarded): replace `_emit(f"  roles: {roles_txt}")` and `_emit("  set with /context <size> …")` with `line = f"  …"; _emit(line if not _RICH else Text(line, style=_DIM))` (do NOT use note() — it prepends "  · ", changing the text). For show_models' bindings block, render the caption dim the same way and the rows via the shared listing vocabulary: `from .listing import table; table([[role, mid] for role, mid in bindings.items()], styles=["dim", None])` — note this intentionally changes the plain-text shape (adds the `│` rail glyph + standard column alignment), matching every other listing; the "output text unchanged" constraint only holds for the dim-styling half. No tests pin the old output; extend tests/test_tui_polish.py with a capture asserting the hint/bindings lines carry the dim style, per the change-one-run-the-suite rule.

title: /docs --help under-reports supported formats as (pdf/txt/md) — html/csv/docx silently missing
description: The /docs details say `add <path>  copy a file into the corpus and embed it (pdf/txt/md)`, but stores/rag.py:22 SUPPORTED_EXTENSIONS is {.txt, .md, .pdf, .html, .htm, .csv, .docx}. A user with a CSV/DOCX/HTML file reads the canonical help and concludes ingestion isn't supported — the shipped loaders (trafilatura HTML, CSV column headers, python-docx) go undiscovered. The runtime error path (line 114) prints the real set, but only if the user tries anyway.
fix: Update the literal in the /docs @command details at commands/knowledge.py:29 to '(txt/md/pdf/html/csv/docx)'. Do NOT build the details string dynamically from SUPPORTED_EXTENSIONS — commands/knowledge.py deliberately lazy-imports stores.rag inside handlers (rag.py imports langchain_core/pypdf/core.llms), and a decoration-time import would drag that surface into command registration at startup. Put the drift guard in tests instead (e.g. tests/test_rag_loaders.py or test_help.py): import both modules there and assert every SUPPORTED_EXTENSIONS suffix (sans dot) appears in the registered /docs command's details string. Optionally fix the same stale '(pdf/txt/md)' in CLAUDE.md's data-layout comment for database/documents/ in the same change.

title: /help claims --help works 'anywhere in its arguments' — the actual grammar is first-or-last only
description: The /help details say 'Every command also accepts a standalone --help / -h token anywhere in its arguments', but dispatch (_framework.py:226) deliberately honors the flag only at the FIRST or LAST argument position (mid-position is data — the documented design, e.g. `/memory add prefer -h over --help in docs` must store the fact). The help text contradicts both the code and CLAUDE.md: a user trusting it could expect `/memory add foo --help bar` to show help when it actually executes and stores text.
fix: In C:\Users\Workstation\Projects\agent\commands\system.py (the /help details string, lines 77-78), replace "Every command also accepts a standalone --help / -h token anywhere in its arguments; it shows this detail view instead of executing" with wording matching the real grammar, e.g.: "Every command also accepts a standalone --help / -h token as its FIRST or LAST argument; it shows this detail view instead of executing (`/trace export --help` explains export, never runs it). A mid-position token is data, so `/memory add prefer -h over --help in docs` stores the fact." No code or test changes needed — the first-or-last behavior is pinned by tests/test_help.py:104-121 (NOT tests/test_command_grammar.py, as the original fix claimed). Optionally extend test_help.py to assert the corrected wording, but the doc reword alone resolves the finding.

title: /dryrun, /policy open, and /plan review silently swallow --save (and any trailing token)
description: parse_toggle_status only inspects args[0], so `/dryrun on --save` flips dry-run on and silently drops the flag — the user believes it persisted (the /config help explicitly advertises --save as 'the same flag every command uses', and sibling toggles /plan lockstep and /privacy airgap honor it) but runtime.dry_run is session-only with no notice. Same silent swallow in open_handler (commands/policy.py:228 — though gate-off is documented never-persisting, it should SAY so when --save is typed) and /plan review (commands/plan.py:75). Any other trailing garbage (`/dryrun on offf`) is likewise ignored.
fix:
In each of the three handlers, run `split_save_flag(args)` (or `args[1:]` for /plan review) before `parse_toggle_status`:
- `/dryrun` (commands/policy.py:646): prefer HONORING the flag rather than the notice — mirror `/privacy airgap` exactly (the closest sibling: same shape of runtime config.yaml leaf + status-bar flag): on explicit on/off + save, `cfg.set` then `_persist_key(cfg, "runtime.dry_run")` (the leaf exists at config.yaml:79); on bare `--save`, persist the CURRENT value per the shared convention (never refuse, never flip). Persisted dry-run fails safe (nothing executes), so this is strictly less dangerous than the already-honored airgap persist.
- `open_handler` (policy.py:222): when save is present, print an explicit "gate-off is session-only — it never persists; --save ignored" line (do NOT persist; the /autoapprove details at policy.py:605 make never-persisting deliberate). This covers `/policy open`, `/autoapprove`, and `/yolo` automatically (one handler).
- `/plan review` (plan.py:74): same explicit session-only notice (ctx.review_plan is loop state, not a config key).
The optional `parse_toggle_status` hardening (return "invalid" when tokens remain after args[0]) is worth doing — `/policy open on garbage` opening the gate is the worst instance, and fail-closed-to-usage matches the gate's design — but it must land AFTER the three handlers split `--save` first (lockstep/airgap already do, so they're unaffected), and per CLAUDE.md's rule extend tests in the same change: add `/dryrun on --save` (persists), `/policy open on --save` (notice + no persist), `/plan review on --save` (notice), and `on <garbage>` -> usage/no-flip cases to tests/test_policy_commands.py and tests/test_plan_context_commands.py. No existing test contradicts any of this.

title: /privacy airgap on hard-codes '/models tier workstation' as the fix for cloud-bound roles
description: When sealing the boundary with cloud-bound roles active, the warning prescribes `switch to a local tier first:  /models tier workstation`. 'workstation' is just one preset name: on a custom config it may not exist, and nothing checks the suggested tier is actually all-local — a hard-coded remediation on the product's flagship privacy flow can point at a 404 (or another cloud tier), while every comparable surface (e.g. /policy can, the doctor) derives its advice live.
fix: In commands/privacy.py, derive the suggestion with a small per-tier resolver over the raw config (cfg.model_for_role only resolves the ACTIVE tier, so _cloud_roles cannot be reused per tier): for each tier name in cfg.get("tiers", {}) in declaration order (declaration order is already meaningful — doctor v2's smallest-first convention), read the tier mapping directly and resolve each declared role's provider the way config.model_for_role does (tier-level `provider` default "ollama"; string entry → tier default; dict entry → entry.get("provider", default)); pick the first tier where every declared role resolves to "ollama" and print `switch to a local tier first:  /models tier <name>`. If none qualifies, fall back to `bare /models tier lists your tiers — pick an all-local one` (accurate: bare `/models tier` lists them, commands/runtime.py). In the same change, fix the sibling hard-coded hint in core/llms.py:162 (the air-gap RuntimeError) — since core/ shouldn't import commands/, either replace it with the generic wording ("Switch to an all-local tier — `/models tier` lists them") or move the derive helper somewhere both can use (e.g. config.py). Add a test asserting the derived suggestion on a config whose tiers are custom-named (no "workstation").

title: A typo'd /trace subcommand falls through to a full drill-down of the last run instead of erroring
description: The _trace dispatcher matches subcommands by exact token (lines 967-992); anything unrecognized falls into the run-selector loop, which prints 'ignoring unrecognized argument' per token (line 1013) and then proceeds to expand the LAST run. So `/trace exprot run_7.json` or `/trace verfy file.json` scrolls two ignore lines followed by a screenful of the wrong thing — the warning is immediately buried by the unrequested replay. This contradicts the repo's own 2026-06-11 grammar ruling for /mcp ('a typo'd relod must not look like a reload'): an unknown verb should stop, not degrade into the default view.
fix: In _trace's fallback loop (commands/trace.py:1013), error out only when the unrecognized token is the FIRST argument (the subcommand position): inside the else branch, if `a == args[0]` (track the index, don't compare values — a duplicate token later is fine), print the repo-standard phrasing `unknown /trace subcommand {a!r} — usage: /trace [#id | -l | why | answer | invoke | calls | cost | state | export | verify | replay | key | on|off|full]` and return; later-position unknown tokens keep the existing per-token ignore line (matching the post-verb flag-tail behavior at lines 405/1084, which stay unchanged). Do NOT implement this as a pre-loop 'non-flag/non-numeric/non-#' check on args[0]: `list` and `ls` are valid bare first tokens recognized inside the loop (line 1000, the LIST_VERBS grammar CLAUDE.md applies to `/trace [-l]`), and a pre-loop check would wrongly reject them. Add a test mirroring tests/test_command_grammar.py::test_mcp_unknown_subcommand_errors_instead_of_silent_status (e.g. _trace(ctx, ['exprot', 'run_7.json']) asserts 'unknown' + usage in output and that no run drill-down rendered), plus a guard that LIST_VERBS and bare/#id digits still work — per CLAUDE.md's 'change one of those → extend the suite in the same change'.
